detail:
CONFIRMED genuine. The full failure chain holds end-to-end. (1) SignupView (apps/tenants/auth_views.py:228) mints the post-signup pair with stock RefreshToken.for_user(user). SIMPLE_JWT in config/settings/base.py:141-156 has NO AUTH_TOKEN_CLASSES override, so stock simplejwt RefreshToken is used and its for_user does not inject the custom pw_iat claim (only EmailTokenObtainPairSerializer.get_token at serializers.py:212-226 adds it). (2) User.objects.create_user(password=...) at line 221 is the stock Django UserManager (User is a plain AbstractUser, no manager override), which calls the OVERRIDDEN set_password (models.py:96-107) stamping password_last_changed_at=timezone.now() in memory, then does a full user.save() — so the stamp is persisted (no update_fields to drop it). The field defaults null, but after signup it is non-null. (3) The default auth class is JWTAuthenticationWithRLS (base.py:131-134); its authenticate (authentication.py:74-87) sees password_last_changed_at is not None, computes token_pw_iat = int(token.get('pw_iat') or 0) = 0, and since 0 < int(password_last_changed_at.timestamp()) is always true for a just-created user, raises AuthenticationFailed(code='password_rotated'). Net effect: the access/refresh pair returned by POST /api/v1/auth/signup/ cannot authenticate ANY protected endpoint. A brand-new email/password signup gets 201 with tokens, then is bounced with the confusing 'Session expired due to a security update. Please sign in again.' message on its very first authenticated call (e.g. /auth/me/, onboarding) — the signup funnel is broken. This is the exact regression class that PasswordResetConfirmView already fixed by switching to EmailTokenObtainPairSerializer.get_token (auth_views.py:170-174, with an explicit code comment about it) and that the OAuth path has a regression guard for (test_oauth_exchange.py). SignupView was missed. The signup unit tests (tests.py:125-163) only assert 201 plus presence of 'access'/'refresh' keys — none exercises the returned token against a protected endpoint, so the gap is untested. Caveat on real-world reach: this fires only on the email/password signup path; if the production frontend signup funnel is gated behind PREVIEW_ACCESS_KEY/invite or signup is currently low-traffic, blast radius is limited — but for anyone who does sign up via email/password, it is a hard 100% break.</final_detail>
<parameter name="suggested_fix">Mint the post-signup pair the same way PasswordResetConfirmView and OAuthTokenExchange do: replace `refresh = RefreshToken.for_user(user)` at apps/tenants/auth_views.py:228 with `refresh = EmailTokenObtainPairSerializer.get_token(user)` (import is already available in the module via serializers). That injects pw_iat = int(password_last_changed_at.timestamp()), which equals the stamp set during create_user, so the auth check 0-vs-timestamp passes. Add a regression test in AuthSignupTest that takes the returned access token and hits a protected endpoint (e.g. /auth/me/) expecting 200, mirroring test_oauth_exchange.py:74-99.

detail:
GENUINE — the fix is incomplete and reintroduces the original FA-0510 wrong-item write on common to-do shapes. I reproduced this empirically against the exact installed parser (react-markdown 10.1.0 / remark-gfm 4.0.1 / remark-breaks 4.0.0 in frontend/node_modules). The fix (markdown-renderer.tsx:21,37-51,66) maps the Nth rendered checkbox <input> to taskLineIndices[N], where taskLineIndices is built by a regex (TASK_LINE_RE) scan of the raw markdown. This only works if the regex flags EXACTLY the lines remark-gfm renders as checkboxes, in the same order. It does not. Of 6 realistic inputs tested, 5 diverged: (1) empty middle task "- [x] real
- [ ] 
- [x] after
" → regex [0,1,2] but parser renders only lines [0,2]; (2) blockquote task → regex [2], parser [0,2]; (3) "[x]done" no space + text → regex [0,1], parser [1]; (4) 4-space indented (code block) → regex [0,1], parser [1]; (5) trailing-space empty task at EOF → regex [0,1], parser [0]. Only a clean valid list matched. Any divergence shifts every subsequent ordinal. I traced the empty-middle-task case end to end: clicking the visible "after" checkbox (real source line 2) consumes ordinal 1 → taskLineIndices[1]=1 → handleCheckboxToggle (document-view.tsx:357-378) rewrites line 1 (the empty "- [ ] " task) to "- [x] " and PATCH-persists it. User clicks one item, a different item is toggled and saved — the exact silent wrong-item mutation FA-0510 was meant to kill, via a new mechanism. The live path is confirmed wired (document-view.tsx:543: onCheckboxToggle passed for every non-typedManaged journal doc). Compounding the issue: the fix_dispositions.json summary CLAIMS the fix reads node.position.start.line from the AST (the robust approach the original suggested_fix recommended first), but the committed code's own comment (lines 32-36) admits the <input> AST node has no position and falls back to the regex-ordinal scheme. I verified in the hast pipeline that the <input> node indeed carries position=undefined, while the enclosing <li> node DOES carry the correct per-checkbox source line (1,2,3) — i.e. the truly correct fix (use the <li> node's position) was available and was missed. User impact: any journal/daily-note containing an empty/placeholder task line ("- [ ] " with a trailing space, a very common authoring artifact, including the trailing-space-at-EOF case), a blockquoted task, indented task text, or "[x]text" without a space will mis-route checkbox clicks for all later checkboxes in that document — silently toggling and persisting the wrong task.
suggested_fix: Derive the checkbox's source line from the enclosing list-item AST node, not from a regex and not from the <input> node. In react-markdown, override the `li` component (or pass the parent context) and read node.position.start.line (1-based) for that list item, converting to 0-based, then forward that exact line to onCheckboxToggle. The hast `li` node reliably carries position (verified: lines 1,2,3 for a 3-item list) whereas the synthetic `input` node does not. This removes the regex-vs-grammar divergence entirely. If staying with an `input`-level override, walk to the owning `li`'s position via the render tree rather than relying on document-order ordinals. Add a test covering empty tasks ("- [ ] " trailing space), blockquote tasks, indented/code-block lines, and "[x]text" to lock the alignment.

detail: CONFIRMED genuine. In production no BYOCredential ever reaches VERIFIED. upsert_credential() (services.py:130-140) hardcodes status=PENDING on every paste/re-paste, and a repo-wide grep finds Status.VERIFIED set ONLY in tests, never in app code. There is no background verifier (the frontend admits this at byo-provider-card.tsx:52-54). Critically, the rest of the system treats PENDING as the live/working state: apply_byo_credentials_to_container (azure_client.py:1148) and the model-extras resolver (config_generator.py:1004) both .exclude(status=ERROR) only, so a PENDING cred IS applied to the container and DOES enable the Claude models. So in prod every real, working credential is permanently PENDING. mark_credential_error() (services.py:186-190) selects the cred with .exclude(status=PENDING), which can never match a real cred, so it always returns None. The entire error-surfacing loop is dead in prod: RuntimeBYOErrorReportView (runtime_views.py:2209-2228) calls mark_credential_error, gets None, logs 'no matching cred', returns 200 'no_credential'; the cred is never flipped to ERROR; BYOProviderCard only renders the rose banner / Reconnect CTA when status is 'error' or 'expired' (byo-provider-card.tsx:57,66,109). User impact: when a tenant's Claude subscription runs out of extra usage or the OAuth token expires/is revoked, the turn fails and the runtime reports it, but nothing is recorded -- the user never sees the 'out of extra usage -- top up' / 'reconnect' banner this feature was built to show, and the AI Provider card shows a perpetual 'Pending verification' pill with a 'Cancel' CTA even for a long-working connection (byo-provider-card.tsx:47,55). The masking is real: integrations/tests.py:143 seeds the cred as VERIFIED (a state that never occurs in prod) so test_billing_error_flips_credential_to_error_status passes; and byo_models/tests.py:605-621 even encodes the wrong intent, asserting that a PENDING cred is NOT flipped (assertIsNone), with the comment 'Pending creds shouldn't get flipped to error -- they haven't finished their first verification.' That comment reveals the false premise: it assumes a verifier will advance PENDING->VERIFIED, but none exists, so the guard permanently excludes every live cred. This is not intended behaviour or out-of-scope -- it is a logic bug masked by tests seeding an impossible-in-prod state.
suggested_fix: In mark_credential_error() (apps/byo_models/services.py:186-190), stop excluding PENDING. Since PENDING is the de-facto working state in Phase 1 (no verifier), the runtime error report should be allowed to flip a PENDING (or VERIFIED) cred to ERROR; only already-ERROR/EXPIRED creds are uninteresting. Simplest correct change: drop the .exclude(status=PENDING) entirely (idempotent flip; re-flipping ERROR to ERROR is harmless), or narrow it to .exclude(status__in=[ERROR]) if you want to avoid redundant writes. Then fix the masking tests: change integrations/tests.py:143 and byo_models/tests.py to seed PENDING (the real prod state) and assert the cred IS flipped to ERROR; update/remove the byo_models/tests.py:605-621 test whose premise is wrong. Optionally, until a real verifier exists, surface PENDING in the UI as 'Connected' rather than 'Pending verification' so the working state reads correctly.

detail:
GENUINE config/env-stranding gap. The core defect holds; one tangential side-claim by the reviewer is wrong.

Core defect: when OpenClaw reports a BYO billing/auth failure, RuntimeBYOErrorReportView.post (apps/integrations/runtime_views.py:2167-2230) calls ONLY mark_credential_error. It never calls tenant.bump_pending_config(), regenerate_tenant_config(), or apply_byo_credentials_to_container(). This is asymmetric with the paste path (apps/byo_models/views.py:136-153) and the delete path (views.py:178-193), both of which deliberately do all three so the live container openclaw.json and env stay consistent with cred state.

At paste time the container is forced into a BYO-only state: openclaw.json pins the Claude primary with an INTENTIONALLY EMPTY fallback list (config_generator.py:1064-1067 -- the empty fallback is by design so a billing failure surfaces instead of dropping to a metered model) and apply_byo_credentials_to_container (azure_client.py:1176-1184) strips ANTHROPIC_API_KEY and binds CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN. After the error report flips the cred to ERROR, the SOURCE state would self-heal IF regenerated: _byo_model_extras (config_generator.py:999-1006) and apply_byo_credentials_to_container (azure_client.py:1143-1150) both exclude ERROR creds, so a regen would repopulate metered fallbacks AND restore ANTHROPIC_API_KEY. But nothing on the error path triggers that regen, so the running container keeps the stale openclaw.json and stale env. The hourly apply-pending-configs cron skips this tenant (config_version >= pending_config_version, tasks.py:171; the error path never bumped pending). The runtime entrypoint only reconciles at boot. No dedicated BYO error-reconcile cron exists -- the only apps/cron BYO endpoints (rollout-byo-image-bump / rollout-byo-persona-refresh, cron/views.py:1561+) are manual workflow_dispatch rollouts that do not touch a failed cred.

User impact: after a user's Claude subscription hits a billing/auth failure, their assistant goes dark for ALL turns, not just the failing one. The primary model cannot be called, there is no fallback to a platform model, and ANTHROPIC_API_KEY was stripped from the container. Recovery is only incidental (any unrelated config bump, image rollout, or the user re-pasting/disconnecting the cred triggers a regen that self-heals) -- there is no automatic recovery on the failure itself.

CORRECTION to the reviewer's secondary claim: the reviewer asserts mark_credential_error returns None today because of a PENDING-exclusion bug, so even the cred-status flip does not happen. That is WRONG. A live, working BYO cred is in VERIFIED status (paste leaves it PENDING at services.py:136, but it becomes VERIFIED once verified; the test at apps/integrations/tests.py:138-144 seeds VERIFIED and asserts the flip to ERROR succeeds at lines 170-173). mark_credential_error excludes only PENDING (services.py:186-190), so a VERIFIED cred IS matched and DOES flip to ERROR, and the rose error banner DOES surface. So the user IS told to reconnect/disconnect (which is exactly what triggers self-heal). The cred-status/banner side of the loop works; only the live container config/env reconcile is missing. Severity stays high because the assistant is fully unresponsive until the user acts on that banner.
suggested_fix: On the actionable-failure branch of RuntimeBYOErrorReportView.post (after mark_credential_error returns a non-None cred), mirror the paste/delete coupling: call tenant.bump_pending_config(), regenerate_tenant_config(tenant), and apply_byo_credentials_to_container(tenant), each wrapped in try/except so a failure is logged but still returns 200 (the runtime should not retry). Because _byo_model_extras and apply_byo_credentials_to_container both exclude ERROR creds, this regenerates openclaw.json with a working metered primary + real fallbacks and restores ANTHROPIC_API_KEY in the container env, so the assistant keeps responding on a platform model while the user reconnects. Optionally add a lightweight periodic BYO-reconcile cron as a backstop for tenants whose error-path regen failed, since the apply-pending-configs cron will not pick them up without a pending bump.

detail: Confirmed against the codebase's own authoritative contract. nbhd_gravity_query (runtime/openclaw/plugins/nbhd-finance-tools/index.js:529) is defined `async execute(args) { ... body: args }`, reading args[0]. But the codebase's own tool-logger establishes the canonical OpenClaw execute signature as execute(toolCallId, params, signal, onUpdate) -- params is args[1], NOT args[0]. This is asserted in several independent places: the docblock example at tool-logger.js:36 (execute(id, params)), the paramsFromArgs comment at tool-logger.js:67-77 which explicitly names "finance gravity_query" as a legacy args[0] tool, the SDK-citing comment at tool-logger.js:134-139, and the dedicated tests at tool-logger.test.js:342-378. Decisively, EVERY other tool in the same finance file (8 of them: lines 173/217/269/310/342/374/417/446) plus the sibling nbhd_journal_query (nbhd-journal-tools/index.js:1806) use the correct execute(_id, params) with asObject(params); gravity_query at line 529 is the ONLY tool across all plugins using the single-arg execute(args) form. So args binds to the toolCallId string. callRuntime then JSON.stringify(body) (line 82) serializes that string and POSTs it. On the backend, query_view.py:138 does self.query_model(**(request.data or {})); request.data is now a Python str, and **"call_..." raises TypeError: argument after ** must be a mapping, not str -- which is NOT a pydantic ValidationError (the only caught exception, line 139), so it is an uncaught 500. Every nbhd_gravity_query call fails before reaching the DB. Note: tool-logger's paramsFromArgs guard tolerates both arg positions but only for the required-arg PRESENCE check -- it does not rewrite the tool's execute body, so the actual HTTP call still ships the toolCallId string; the guard passing does not save the call. The Python FinanceQueryView tests exercise the view with a well-formed dict and never touch the plugin wiring, and there are no JS tests for this plugin, so nothing catches it. The same args[0] mis-wiring exists in nbhd-image-gen (index.js:159, execute({prompt,size})), which the tool-logger comment also flags as legacy -- consistent with this being a known un-fixed pattern, not intended behavior; journal_query was deliberately written the correct way later. User impact: the system prompt (index.js:459-496) MANDATES nbhd_gravity_query for every quantitative finance claim under a GROUNDING CONTRACT that forbids reciting numbers from memory/USER.md. With the tool erroring on every call, all targeted finance queries (last payment to a given account, this week's payments, payoff-progress, debt totals via aggregate) are dead; the assistant either dead-ends ("can't read your finances") or violates the grounding contract and recites stale USER.md figures. The older fixed-snapshot nbhd_finance_summary still works, so finance is degraded, not fully dark -- hence high (not critical).
suggested_fix: Change line 529 from `async execute(args) {` to `async execute(_id, params) {` and line 533 from `body: args,` to `body: asObject(params),` -- exactly matching the sibling nbhd_journal_query (nbhd-journal-tools/index.js:1806) and the other 8 finance tools. Optionally add the same fix to nbhd-image-gen/index.js:159 and a defensive `TypeError`/`except Exception` -> 400 around query_view.py:138 so a malformed body can never 500. Add a JS smoke test asserting the body is the params object, not the toolCallId.

detail:
CONFIRMED genuine. apps/pii/redactor.py:392-399 logs a pii_mint record (logger.info with span=%r and original[:32]) on every newly-minted entity inside _redact_user_message. original is the RAW detected PII span (out[result.start:result.end]); the [:32] slice only caps length while %r renders the cleartext value verbatim. It fires for every entity in TIER_POLICIES['starter']['entities'] (config.py:87-103): PERSON, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PHONE_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD, IBAN_CODE, PASSWORD, IP_ADDRESS, ID_DOCUMENT, ACCOUNT, CRYPTO_ADDRESS. A 16-digit Luhn-validated PAN, most IBANs, emails, phone numbers, contact names, and most passwords all fit within 32 chars and are therefore logged in full.

Delivery confirmed: logger is getLogger(__name__) = apps.pii.redactor, which has no dedicated logger config and propagates to root (level INFO, console StreamHandler, {message} formatter) per production.py:101-126, so the message renders to stdout and ships to Azure Container Apps Log Analytics. The only scrubbing filter, redact_byo_paste_body (byo_models/logging_filters.py:39-50), early-returns True unless a record field contains the literal path /api/v1/tenants/byo-credentials/; pii_mint records never carry that path, so they pass through untouched.

This is a live hot path, not dead/test code: redact_user_message -> _redact_user_message runs on every inbound user message across all channels — Telegram poller (router/poller.py:1374), LINE webhook (router/line_webhook.py:1248), and iOS/app chat (router/chat_views.py:226). No debug flag, sampling, or env gate guards the call.

Calibration: the leak is operator-facing (Azure Log Analytics, gated behind Azure log-read permission), not internet-public, and it fires only on FIRST detection of each distinct value (the inverted_ci in-message dedup plus the DB-persisted pii_entity_map suppress repeats of an already-minted value). But that still means every new card number, password, IBAN, email, phone number, and contact name lands in cleartext in the production log store the redactor module exists to keep it out of — a direct contradiction of the module's stated purpose and a clear PCI-DSS red flag (storing raw PAN in logs is explicitly prohibited). The FA-0913 inventory's treatment of this as benign tuning telemetry is wrong: score and tenant id are the load-bearing fields; the span text adds nothing for threshold tuning and is the entire leak.
suggested_fix: Stop logging the raw span entirely — it is not needed for the stated "data-driven threshold tuning" purpose (tenant id, entity type, and score are sufficient). Drop the span=%r/original[:32] argument from the logger.info call at redactor.py:392-399. If a coarse signal about span shape is genuinely wanted, log only non-reversible metadata such as len(original) or a salted hash, never the value itself. As defense-in-depth, the redact_byo_paste_body filter could be generalized into a pii_mint-aware filter, but removing the value from the log call is the correct and complete fix.

detail: CONFIRMED genuine. SignupView (apps/tenants/auth_views.py:228) mints the token pair via RefreshToken.for_user(user), which does NOT inject the custom pw_iat claim — only EmailTokenObtainPairSerializer.get_token does (serializers.py:212-226). The force-logout check in JWTAuthenticationWithRLS.authenticate (authentication.py:74-87) then runs on every request: pw_changed_at is not None is True, token_pw_iat = int(token.get('pw_iat') or 0) = 0 (claim absent), and 0 < int(password_last_changed_at.timestamp()) is True, so it raises AuthenticationFailed('Session expired due to a security update. Please sign in again.', code='password_rotated'). Net: signup returns 201 with access+refresh, but those tokens are dead on arrival — the FIRST authenticated request 401s. The codebase documents this exact footgun: PasswordResetConfirmView (auth_views.py:160-174) explicitly mints via get_token 'so the token carries the pw_iat claim — RefreshToken.for_user omits it, and JWTAuthenticationWithRLS then rejects the brand-new token', and oauth_views.py carries the same warning. ExchangeView/PasswordResetConfirmView/the login serializer were all fixed to use get_token; SignupView was missed. EmailTokenObtainPairSerializer is already imported in auth_views.py (line 25), so the fix needs no new import. User impact (frontend/app/signup/page.tsx:37-50): right after signup the client either (a) redirects to /app/authorize — the iOS 'Create an account' web-to-app handoff, a Bearer-authed POST — which 401s, breaking the handoff outright; or (b) calls fetchMe() (GET /me/) which 401s, and even the catch-fallback to /onboarding stays broken because the access token is dead for ALL authenticated requests. Recovery requires logging out and back in (a fresh login re-mints a correct pw_iat token via the serializer path). The existing signup tests (apps/tenants/tests.py) only assert the response contains access/refresh and never replay the token through an authenticated request, so the regression is uncaught. This is a real, user-facing break for every new web signup.
suggested_fix: In SignupView.post, replace `refresh = RefreshToken.for_user(user)` (auth_views.py:228) with `refresh = EmailTokenObtainPairSerializer.get_token(user)` — identical to the already-fixed PasswordResetConfirmView at line 174. The serializer is already imported. Optionally add a regression test that performs an authenticated GET /me/ (or similar) using the token returned from signup to assert it does not 401.

detail: The reviewer is correct. The fix's own disposition states "the Horizons response carries the goal's full markdown in `preview`" — that premise is factually false. apps/dashboard/views.py:373 serializes goals[].preview as `(g["markdown"] or "")[:200]`, a hard 200-char truncation. The new detail page (frontend/app/journal/goal/[slug]/goal-detail-client.tsx:121) renders exactly that field via `<MarkdownRenderer content={goal.preview} />`. The HorizonsGoal type (frontend/lib/types.ts:581-588) exposes only id/title/slug/preview/created_at/updated_at — no full-content field — and the response (views.py:368-378) never emits the underlying `markdown` (typed goals: g.description; legacy: Document.markdown). The page's only data source is useHorizonsQuery → GET /api/v1/dashboard/horizons/; there is no separate goal-detail endpoint. The 200-char limit truncates real goals: the goal template (apps/journal/templates_md.py:97-101) is `### 🎯 [Goal Name]` + Target/Why/Status/Next-milestone labels — roughly 190 chars of scaffolding before any user content — so any fully-populated goal exceeds 200 chars and the detail page shows a mid-sentence stub. User impact: the routing dead-end (the original high defect) IS fixed — the in-app click now lands on a real page showing the title and a partial body — but the "View in Journal" CTA does not deliver the full goal; users see the same truncated snippet the card already shows, with no way to read the rest. It is an incomplete fix, not a broken one (title + ~200 chars still render), which is why I rate it medium rather than high.
suggested_fix: Expose the full goal markdown to the detail view rather than reusing the 200-char preview. Minimal change: add a `markdown` (full body) field to the goals[] serialization in apps/dashboard/views.py:368-378 (the full text is already in scope as g["markdown"]), add it to the HorizonsGoal type, and have goal-detail-client.tsx render `goal.markdown` (falling back to goal.preview). The list cards continue using the truncated `preview`; only the detail page needs the full field. Adding ~a few hundred chars per goal (capped at 20 goals) to the existing Horizons payload is negligible.

detail:
GENUINE incomplete fix. The fix for FA-0533 closed only the three named 'accounts' cases (order_by=is_debt, order_by=payoff_progress, group_by=is_debt) by carving them out of the order-by/group-by allowlists, with 7 accounts-only tests in tests_audit_C30.py. It left an identical FieldError->HTTP 500 hole on the 'transactions' resource in the very same _order() method.

Reproduction path (verified against the code):
- query_views.py:332 — `_order` resolves the order-by allowlist as `_ALLOWED_ORDER_BY.get(resource, _ALLOWED_FIELDS[resource])`. `_ALLOWED_ORDER_BY` only has an 'accounts' key (line 128-130), so 'transactions' falls back to `_ALLOWED_FIELDS['transactions']`.
- query_views.py:85-94 — `_ALLOWED_FIELDS['transactions']` includes `account_nickname`.
- account_nickname is NOT a column on FinanceTransaction. models.py:94-119 shows the model's only fields are id, tenant, account (FK), transaction_type, amount, description, date, created_at. The serializer derives account_nickname from `t.account.nickname` (query_views.py:417) — a relation traversal, not a field.
- query_views.py:333 — `account_nickname` is in the (fallback) allowlist, so the membership check passes.
- query_views.py:338 — reaches `qs.order_by('account_nickname')`, where Django raises django.core.exceptions.FieldError (the join field would be `account__nickname`, not `account_nickname`).
- apps/common/query_view.py:148-155 — `BaseQueryView.post` catches ONLY QueryExecutionError. No custom DRF EXCEPTION_HANDLER is configured (confirmed: zero matches in config/), so DRF's default handler does not convert FieldError, and it propagates to a Django 500.

So POST resource=transactions, order_by=account_nickname returns HTTP 500 — exactly the strict-400-contract violation FA-0533 was about. This is a natural agent query (transactions sorted by account name). The original defect explicitly named this invariant ("order-by allowlist must reference real DB columns") and explicitly recommended a belt-and-suspenders defense: wrap ORM execution to translate FieldError into QueryExecutionError(400). That defense was NOT applied, so the whole FieldError->500 class remains reachable; the fix papered over the two specifically-named accounts members instead of fixing the class. User impact is bounded: agent-only internal read surface (nbhd_gravity_query), no data corruption, just a 500 instead of a clean 400.
suggested_fix: Two-part, matching the original defect's own suggested_fix: (1) Add a `transactions` (and `plan`) entry to `_ALLOWED_ORDER_BY` listing only real DB columns — for transactions that is {id, account_id, transaction_type, amount, description, date, created_at} (drop account_nickname), so order_by=account_nickname returns the existing clean invalid_order_by 400; keep account_nickname in `_ALLOWED_FIELDS`/group_by where it is special-cased to account__nickname. (2) Belt-and-suspenders: in BaseQueryView.post (or _order/_aggregate) catch django.core.exceptions.FieldError and re-raise as QueryExecutionError("invalid_order_by"/"invalid_query", status 400) so any future serializer-only field can never produce a 500. Add a test: POST resource=transactions, window={kind:all}, order_by=account_nickname -> 400.

detail: GENUINE incomplete fix. The FA-0584 fix (commit c1124d2) correctly fixed the ORIGINAL defect (PATCH now runs _validate_normalize_schedule before persisting schedule_json). But it ALSO bundled a brand-new PATCH-writable week_overrides block, and that addition is incomplete. Git confirms the week_overrides PATCH block at runtime_views.py:1389-1395 did not exist before c1124d2 (week_overrides was not a writable PATCH field). The block validates overrides via _validate_normalize_week_overrides, assigns plan.week_overrides, sets needs_regeneration=True, and returns HTTP 200 -- but the regen path it triggers cannot consume week_overrides. At runtime_views.py:1431-1433 PATCH calls reconcile_plan_state(plan, plan.schedule_json, plan.weeks, today=...) plus apply_reconciliation, neither of which receives or reads week_overrides. Confirmed by grep: apps/fuel/services.py has ZERO references to week_overrides. The reconciler builds ONE weekday-keyed template via _parse_schedule_template(schedule_json) (services.py:127) and applies it identically to every week (for w in range(weeks) at services.py:139; template_by_weekday.get(...) at lines 203 and 250) -- there is no week dimension, so per-week deloads (different detail/category/rpe for week N) and per-week rest-day drops (a day mapped to null) are structurally inexpressible on this path. Only the POST/create path honors them, via _expand_plan_workouts(..., week_overrides=normalized_overrides) at runtime_views.py:1284 (per-week merge plus null-day drop at lines 1050-1059). Net effect: create-with-overrides materializes correctly; PATCH-with-overrides stores AND round-trips the overrides (_serialize_plan includes week_overrides at line 872) and reports 200 success, but the materialized Workout calendar is unchanged. The fix disposition advertises week_overrides validation for POST parity, which is a half-truth: it delivers VALIDATION parity but NOT MATERIALIZATION parity. The claim's empirical repro (PATCH 200, overrides stored, but Week2-Mon still Heavy Squats rpe None instead of Deload Squats rpe 5, and the rest-day Wed not dropped) is fully consistent with the code. USER/OPERATOR IMPACT: this is a runtime/OpenClaw assistant-facing endpoint. A user asking the assistant to make week 3 a deload or add a rest day to an existing periodized plan via PATCH gets a 200 success and a confirmation, but the calendar silently does not change -- a real, user-reachable data-integrity/UX defect, though bounded (periodized-plan edits only, no crash, no security impact). The only test (tests.py:3669 test_week_overrides_progression_and_rest) exercises POST only; no PATCH-materialization coverage exists.
suggested_fix: Thread week_overrides through the reconcile path so PATCH materialization matches POST. Concretely: (1) add a week_overrides param to reconcile_plan_state and have it select the effective per-week template (base schedule merged with week_overrides[str(week_offset)], dropping days mapped to null) when computing desired_keys, workouts_to_create, and workouts_to_retemplate -- i.e. resolve template per (week_index, weekday) rather than per weekday only; (2) pass plan.week_overrides from the PATCH call site at runtime_views.py:1431-1433; (3) ensure rest-day drops (null) archive the corresponding slot/workout for that specific week. Alternatively, if the reconciler is too costly to make week-aware, have the PATCH path fall back to the POST-style _expand_plan_workouts regeneration whenever week_overrides is present (accepting the slot-identity churn trade-off). Add a PATCH-materialization test mirroring tests.py:3669 (PATCH week_overrides, assert calendar dates and the deload activity/rpe).

detail:
CONFIRMED incomplete fix. The added line (apps/orchestrator/services.py:616) uploads templates.md to the file share on config refresh and does correctly reach a currently-running container — but the write is non-durable and is clobbered on the next container boot, so the original FA-0907 failure mode recurs after any restart/wake.

The full chain is verified in code:
1. The nbhd-managed skills tree lives on the mounted file share: volume 'workspace' is mounted at /home/node/.openclaw (azure_client.py:847), and SKILL_TEMPLATES_DST = $OPENCLAW_WORKSPACE_PATH/skills/nbhd-managed/daily-journal/references/templates.md is on that share.
2. runtime/openclaw/entrypoint.sh:15-19 unconditionally `rm -rf "$NBHD_MANAGED_SKILLS_DST"` then `cp -R` the IMAGE default skills on EVERY boot (the guard is just `[ -d "$NBHD_MANAGED_SKILLS_SRC" ]`, which the image always satisfies). This destroys the share copy the fix uploaded.
3. entrypoint.sh:30-35 then rewrites templates.md from the `NBHD_SKILL_TEMPLATES_MD` env var.
4. `NBHD_SKILL_TEMPLATES_MD` is a provision-time snapshot: it is part of `workspace_env` set only at container create (services.py:337-348 -> azure_client.py:844 spreads workspace_env into env). `update_tenant_config` (services.py:570-649) only writes to the share via upload_workspace_file; it NEVER calls update_container_env_var. grep confirms update_container_env_var is called only by disable_tenant_telegram_polling.py (Telegram token disable), never for NBHD_SKILL_TEMPLATES_MD.
5. A plain hibernation wake (hibernation.py:522 wake_container_app) only queues apply_single_tenant_config when version_synced or config_version==0 (hibernation.py:544/550). On the common plain-wake path nothing re-uploads templates.md, so the boot clobber is permanent until the next default-template edit fires update_tenant_config_task again.

The trigger source is real and user-reachable: NoteTemplate PATCH/DELETE on the default template (journal/views.py:505-509, 531-535) fires update_tenant_config_task; render_templates_md (personas.py:322-356) emits the slug list from the user's live template sections — exactly the slug list FA-0907 says goes stale.

User/operator impact: after a user changes their default daily-note template sections and then the container restarts or wakes from hibernation (the dominant lifecycle here — 1 active + 23 suspended waking on reactivation), templates.md reverts to the stale provision-time slug list. The assistant then calls nbhd_daily_note_set_section with stale/wrong slugs -> wrong-section writes or errors, i.e. the exact FA-0907 defect recurs. Requires (template edit) AND (restart/wake), so not constant, hence medium not high. Note: even on wakes that DO re-apply, there is a boot-vs-apply timing race where the entrypoint's stale-env write can run after the apply and win.

The reviewer's non-regression analysis is also correct: the change is purely additive to file_map_overwrite, render_workspace_files always sets the key when tenant is provided, update_tenant_config always passes tenant, render_templates_md always returns non-empty so the `if content:` guard handles the no-template case, and no test asserts the dict contents.
suggested_fix: Make the share copy authoritative OR keep the env var in sync. Cleanest: stop seeding templates.md from the boot env var — remove the entrypoint.sh:29-35 block (and ideally narrow the rm -rf at :15-19 so it does not delete the daily-journal/references/templates.md the share already holds, or restore it from the share rather than the image default). Then the file-share copy written by update_tenant_config is never clobbered. Alternatively, on config refresh also call azure_client.update_container_env_var(container_id, "NBHD_SKILL_TEMPLATES_MD", render_templates_md(tenant)) inside update_tenant_config so the boot-time snapshot the entrypoint rewrites from stays current — but this still leaves the boot-vs-apply race and triggers a revision bump, so the entrypoint-decoupling approach is preferred.

detail:
CONFIRMED in code. reset_monthly_counters() (apps/tenants/services.py:81-85) zeroes estimated_cost_this_month/tokens_this_month/messages_this_month only for tenants matching messages_this_month__gt=0. But those counters accrue under DIFFERENT conditions in record_usage (apps/billing/services.py:219-230): messages_this_month bumps by msg_increment = (message_count if event_type=='message' else 0) at line 221, while estimated_cost_this_month bumps by cost for EVERY non-system, non-BYO event regardless of event_type at line 229 (gated only by `not is_system`). So a tenant with cost>0 and messages_this_month==0 is structurally reachable and is SKIPPED by the reset filter, carrying last month's cost into the new month. The test at test_credits.py:155-156 encodes exactly this assumption: it forces messages_this_month=3 so the reset fires, commented 'reset_monthly_counters only resets tenants who sent messages'.

The strongest real, scheduled accrual path is the OpenRouter reconcile cron (reconcile_openrouter_spend.py:74), verified registered as an hourly system cron (register_system_crons.py:43 = '10 * * * *'). It updates estimated_cost_this_month upward from provider truth (OR usage_monthly) and never touches messages_this_month. Compounding it: the reset cron runs '5 0 1 * *' (00:05 on the 1st) while reconcile runs hourly at :10. At 00:10 on the 1st, if OR usage_monthly has not yet rolled over to the new calendar month, get_key_usage returns last month's total as provider_truth; the 'update upward only' guard (provider_truth <= before, line 67) sees before=0 just after reset and RE-INFLATES estimated_cost_this_month back to last month's full spend, bypassing the message filter entirely. _reconcile_platform_total (line 111) then sums stale per-tenant values into MonthlyBudget.spent_dollars (line 123), nudging the global breaker.

Impact is real: is_over_budget / has_spendable_budget (models.py:773-789) enforce the included cap on estimated_cost_this_month, so an affected tenant can be throttled or have prepaid credit drawn at the start of a month they have spent nothing in.

Two caveats lower the severity from the claimed 'high' to medium: (a) the second cited path (the runtime usage endpoint at runtime_views.py:2087-2118 accepting arbitrary event_type) is currently LATENT, not exercised — the deployed in-fleet usage-reporter plugin hardcodes event_type:'message' (runtime/openclaw/plugins/nbhd-usage-reporter/index.js:152), so today only the reconcile cron actually accrues message-free cost; (b) any tenant who sent a normal message that month already has messages_this_month>0 and resets correctly, so the pure filter-narrowness strand requires the unusual zero-message-but-billed tenant. Still a genuine correctness defect: the filter encodes an invariant (cost implies message) that the code does not enforce, and the reconcile cross-boundary re-inflation makes it concretely reachable.
suggested_fix: Drop the message-count predicate from the cost/token reset. Either reset unconditionally, or filter on the actual counters being reset. Replace apps/tenants/services.py:81 filter(messages_this_month__gt=0).update(...) with a filter that ORs all three counters, e.g. Tenant.objects.filter(Q(messages_this_month__gt=0) | Q(estimated_cost_this_month__gt=0) | Q(tokens_this_month__gt=0)).update(messages_this_month=0, tokens_this_month=0, estimated_cost_this_month=0). Separately harden the cross-boundary re-inflation in reconcile_openrouter_spend: skip/clamp when get_key_usage's provider_truth predates the reset (e.g. don't true-up within the first minutes of the month, or track an OR billing-period marker), so a just-zeroed counter isn't refilled from last month's usage_monthly. Update test_credits.py MonthlyResetTest to also assert a cost-only / zero-message tenant gets reset.

detail:
Confirmed against the code. The global platform kill-switch is implemented entirely via the MonthlyBudget.spent_dollars side-effect, NOT the is_capped boolean. Enforcement at apps/billing/services.py:288 reads `global_budget.remaining <= 0` (remaining = budget_dollars - spent_dollars, models.py:142-143); it never consults is_capped. is_capped has zero enforcement readers (writers are only cap_budget.py lines 51-52/57-58; not even read by __str__, which prints spent/budget). So is_capped is a dead flag.

cap_budget --uncap (cap_budget.py:50) sets spent_dollars = 0. But _reconcile_platform_total (reconcile_openrouter_spend.py:120-123) runs hourly ('10 * * * *' per register_system_crons.py:43) and ratchets spent_dollars upward toward provider truth: `if platform_truth > current_spent: spent_dollars += delta`. It only ever increases spent_dollars.

Two verified consequences: (a) NON-DURABLE UNCAP: after --uncap, if true month-to-date platform spend already exceeds budget_dollars, the next hourly reconcile re-derives spent_dollars >= budget_dollars, so remaining <= 0 re-engages and check_budget returns 'global' again — every tenant silently re-blocked within the hour despite the operator's explicit uncap. (b) LOST SPEND FIGURE: --uncap zeroes spent_dollars, which _get_budget_info (usage_services.py:91) surfaces as global_spent in the transparency/usage UI; the platform month-to-date spend reads $0 until reconcile re-fills it, then jumps back to truth.

Operator impact: someone following the documented cap_budget --uncap path to 'resume service' is misled — the resume is non-durable if real spend is over budget, and the uncap meanwhile wipes the accurate month-to-date spend number. is_capped advertises an independent kill-switch that enforcement never honors.

Minor non-material claim inaccuracies: there is no '--cap' flag (capping is the default no-arg path), and is_capped is not read by __str__ either (the claim said it was). Neither weakens the substance.
suggested_fix: Make is_capped the actual enforcement signal so cap/uncap is an independent, durable switch decoupled from the reconcile-driven spend counter: (1) in check_budget (services.py:~287) return 'global' when `global_budget.is_capped` OR remaining <= 0; (2) stop overloading spent_dollars in cap_budget.py — set/clear is_capped only, and do NOT zero/raise spent_dollars (preserving the true month-to-date figure for the transparency UI); (3) optionally have _reconcile_platform_total set is_capped=True when platform_truth crosses budget so the breaker still auto-engages, but leave operator --uncap authoritative (e.g. uncap also bumps budget_dollars or sets an override window so reconcile doesn't immediately re-flip it). At minimum, decouple the displayed spend (spent_dollars) from the enforcement toggle (is_capped) so --uncap no longer destroys the spend figure and no longer silently re-caps within the hour.

detail: CONFIRMED in code. In RuntimeDailyNoteAppendView.post (apps/integrations/runtime_views.py:1257-1289), when section_slug is supplied the handler derives heading = section_slug.replace('-',' ').title() (line 1262), builds marker = "## " + heading (line 1263), and locates the section with idx = md.find(marker) (line 1265) — an unanchored SUBSTRING search, not line/heading anchored. It preserves the matched heading line (md[:heading_end], where heading_end is the first newline after idx, line 1268) and replaces the body up to the next newline+"## " (lines 1273-1277). Two genuine failure modes. (1) Prefix collision (the cited trace, verified): if a longer heading begins with the marker text, find() matches it. Doc "## Report Card / Grade: A / ## Notes / hello" with section_slug='report' gives marker "## Report", which matches "## Report Card"; heading_end lands after "Card", so the result keeps the "## Report Card" line but destroys "Grade: A", substituting the new content. This requires two sections sharing a heading prefix (e.g. "## Check-in" vs "## Check-in Notes", or inconsistent assistant slugs 'report' then 'report-card'). The three seeded default sections (Morning Report, Log, Evening Check-in) do NOT prefix-collide (migration 0003), so this needs user-customized templates or assistant-created overlapping sections — plausible but not the common case. (2) Body false-match (more likely): both md.find(marker) and the next-heading md.find(newline+"## ") scan the WHOLE document, so a literal "## Heading" or any newline+"## " inside ANOTHER section's body shifts the slice boundaries and splices content into the wrong place or truncates a real section. The assistant routinely writes markdown (including "## " subheadings) into section bodies via this same tool, making spurious matches realistic. This backs nbhd_daily_note_set_section (runtime/openclaw/plugins/nbhd-journal-tools/index.js:390-424 -> POST /daily-note/append/) with assistant-derived slugs, so the assistant can silently clobber the body of a different daily-note section; the loss is journaled content with no error surfaced. Severity is medium, not high: the substring match must be a true prefix of the section-2 heading line (not "## Morning Report" matched by "## Report"), which constrains the prefix-collision path; the body false-match is broader but tends to mangle one note rather than fleet-wide loss. No anchoring/regex guard exists upstream, and no test covers the collision case (apps/journal/tests.py exercises only distinct section_slugs).
suggested_fix: Anchor the heading match to a full line rather than a substring. Either (a) split md into lines and find the line that exactly equals marker (line.rstrip() == marker), then operate on line ranges; or (b) use a line-anchored regex like re.search(r"(?m)^" + re.escape(marker) + r"\s*$", md) for the start boundary and r"(?m)^## " for the next-heading boundary searched from after the matched line. This makes "## Report" no longer match "## Report Card" and ignores "## " text embedded mid-line inside other section bodies. Add a regression test with two prefix-overlapping headings and a body containing a literal "## " line.

detail:
GENUINE and reproducible. In apps/orchestrator/services.py:264-295 the per-tenant OpenRouter sub-key block is gated ONLY by OPENROUTER_PER_TENANT_KEYS_ENABLED + keyvault backend. There is no skip-if-already-keyed guard (no `if tenant.openrouter_key_hash` / `if not tenant.openrouter_key_secret_name`); it unconditionally calls create_sub_key(...) and overwrites tenant.openrouter_key_secret_name / openrouter_key_hash.

provision_tenant is fully re-runnable into this block. On any failure AFTER the sub-key step (assign_key_vault_role:319, create_tenant_file_share:327, register_environment_storage:329, upload_config:333, create_container_app:342 — all common cold-provisioning failure points), the except handler at 376-386 resets status to PENDING and re-raises. The top-of-function status guard (212-220) only returns when status is NOT in (PENDING, PROVISIONING), so a reset-to-PENDING tenant flows straight back through to re-mint the key. Re-entry is confirmed via two paths: (1) QStash redelivery — provision_tenant is published with the default 3 retries (apps/cron/publish.py:113; tenants/views.py:104,211; billing/services.py:448) and QStash re-POSTs the trigger endpoint when the task raises; (2) repair_stale_tenant_provisioning (services.py:154) re-invokes provision_tenant directly.

Each re-run mints a NEW OpenRouter sub-key with its own independent monthly spend ceiling and overwrites the hash on the Tenant row, so the previous sub-key becomes an orphan that deprovision_tenant cannot reap — deprovision (services.py:705-709) deletes only the single hash currently stored. A tenant that fails provisioning past the OR step accumulates one orphaned sub-key per retry (up to ~3 from QStash plus any repair runs), each silently multiplying the platform's real per-tenant OR spend ceiling.

The codebase already demonstrates awareness of exactly this hazard: backfill_openrouter_keys.py:20-21 is explicitly idempotent ('A tenant that already has a non-empty openrouter_key_secret_name is skipped', enforced by the openrouter_key_secret_name="" queryset filter at line 45) and its comment at 79-82 documents a real 9-orphan incident on the 2026-05-28 fleet backfill. provision_tenant simply lacks that same guard. The test at tests.py:904-926 only asserts create_called_once on a single run; no test asserts a second provision call is a no-op.

MITIGATION (why medium, not high): sweep_orphan_openrouter_keys reaps these orphans by cross-referencing OR sub-key hashes against the Tenant table and DELETEing any unmatched key older than 24h, so the orphans are not permanent — they leak spend-ceiling exposure for up to ~24h plus the sweeper's cadence, and only when the feature flag is on and a post-OR-step failure occurs. That bounds the blast radius but the inflated ceiling is live during that window and the orphans are invisible to the deprovision path.
suggested_fix: Add a skip-if-already-keyed guard at the top of the sub-key block in provision_tenant, mirroring the backfill command's idempotency: wrap the create_sub_key path in `if not tenant.openrouter_key_secret_name:` (or `if not tenant.openrouter_key_hash:`). When already keyed, reuse the existing openrouter_kv_secret_name (set it from tenant.openrouter_key_secret_name so the later assign_key_vault_role grant and create_container_app call still wire the existing secret). Add a regression test asserting a second provision_tenant call on an already-keyed PENDING tenant does NOT call create_sub_key again.

detail:
Confirmed at the code level. Every writer of pii_entity_map does an unsynchronized full-dict read-modify-write with no row lock, no F() expression, and no DB-side JSON merge: redactor (redactor.py:412-414, merged = {**existing_map, **new_map_entries} then update(pii_entity_map=merged)), memory_sync (memory_sync.py:76-83), arbiter (arbiter.py:277-285), and the settings views (views.py:1145 edit, 1172 delete, plus denylist writes 1249/1286). Grep confirms no select_for_update / transaction.atomic / F() / JSONB-merge on any of these writes, and there is no upstream per-tenant lock either: the redactor is invoked from three independent entry points that run as separate processes/requests — poller.py:1374 (Telegram drain), line_webhook.py:1248 (LINE), chat_views.py:226 (iOS chat) — plus the hourly arbiter cron and memory_sync.

Two real failure modes, both verified:
(1) Placeholder-number collision / cross-entity name leak. The per-type counter is derived solely from the snapshot read at function start (redactor.py:323-328 scans existing_map for max N), then count = type_counters.get(etype,0)+1 (line 384). Two concurrent redactions that both read PERSON max=3 each mint [PERSON_4] for DIFFERENT people; the second update() (line 414) wins via {**existing_map, **new} and overwrites the first's [PERSON_4]. Message 1 was already sent to the LLM with [PERSON_4]=person A, but outbound rehydration re-reads the now-live map (pending_queue.py:1516-1521 and 1563-1568 call rehydrate_text on tenant.pii_entity_map) and substitutes person B's real name into the reply about person A — a genuine privacy leak.
(2) Lost write / resurrected delete. A user DELETE (views.py:1171) or an arbiter denylist stamp that lands between a redactor's read and its full-dict write is silently clobbered — the deleted entry resurfaces, or the arbiter judged_at stamp is dropped.

This is correctly distinct from the known FA-0929 case-insensitive 'bloat' merge defects, which over-mint duplicates of the same entity; this finding is about lost writes and cross-entity name substitution from unsynchronized concurrent updates.

Severity is medium, not high. The race is real and the worst case (one contact's real name shown in another's context) is a true privacy leak, but the window is narrow (read-to-write within a single redaction) and the current production fleet is effectively one active, single-user tenant (canary). A single user rarely drives two truly concurrent inbound messages across channels simultaneously, though the hourly arbiter cron and memory_sync running against a live inbound message do widen the window. It is a latent correctness/privacy bug that will bite as the fleet grows or under multi-channel bursts, not a routinely-hit defect today.
suggested_fix: Serialize and atomicize each pii_entity_map mutation per tenant. Minimal: wrap each read-modify-write in transaction.atomic() with Tenant.objects.select_for_update().get(pk=...) to re-read the row under a lock before computing counters and merging, so the counter is derived from a locked snapshot and the merge cannot clobber a concurrent write. Best: move the merge into the DB with a JSONB concat (F('pii_entity_map') with a Postgres '||' merge / RawSQL jsonb_set) so new entries are added without overwriting keys written since the read; combined with select_for_update for the counter derivation, this closes both the collision and the lost-delete paths. Apply the same select_for_update pattern to the four settings views, the arbiter, and memory_sync.

detail:
Confirmed end-to-end. Each inbound iOS message creates one PENDING AppChatMessage (keyed by client_msg_id) AND one PendingMessage row, all with channel=ios and channel_user_id=thread.id (chat_views.py:163-243). When a user fires several messages to the same thread before the first reply lands (the common cold-start burst), _claim_pending_batch_for_key folds the contiguous PENDING rows into ONE coalesced batch — iOS rows are NOT excluded; only voice is forced singleton (pending_queue.py:518-539). _drain_ios_batch sends one combined OpenClaw turn via format_coalesced_user_content and gets ONE reply (pending_queue.py:1403-1432). _store_ios_turn_reply then writes that single text onto EVERY client_msg_id in the batch via .filter(...client_msg_id__in=client_ids).update(reply_text=text, status=READY,...) (pending_queue.py:1466-1476). The feed expands each AppChatMessage to its own assistant row app:<m.id>:1 with text=m.reply_text (chat_history.py:151-167) and dedups only by the synthetic id, never by content; build_since_page iterates per row (chat_history.py:260-264). ChatThreadMessagesView history has the same shape (chat_views.py:294-301 -> _serialize_message at 142). Result: 3 quick messages -> 3 user bubbles followed by the SAME assistant reply repeated 3x. Telegram/LINE escape this because their single coalesced reply is relayed ONCE to the channel API and never fanned out per persisted row. I also verified the secondary impact the reviewer claimed: the USER.md 'Conversation so far' digest reads AppChatMessage directly and appends {user, reply} per row (conversation_capture.py:247-257), so the same assistant text is duplicated N times in the LLM-facing digest. No test exercises iOS coalescing — test_ios_chat.py has no multi-message-into-one-batch case (the coalescing tests in test_pending_queue.py only cover Telegram/LINE relay-once, which is exactly why this per-row fan-out was never caught).</final_detail>
<parameter name="suggested_fix">In _store_ios_turn_reply, attach the combined reply to only ONE representative row of the coalesced batch (e.g. the last client_msg_id) while still flipping the other rows OFF pending so the client stops polling — but not emitting them as assistant rows. Concretely: set reply_text/READY on the representative row, and mark the other rows with a 'merged/answered' status (or set reply_text='' and a status that _app_rows / _serialize_message / the digest treat as 'no assistant row'). _app_rows already only emits the assistant row when status==READY and reply_text is non-empty, so leaving the non-representative rows with empty reply_text and a non-READY-but-terminal status would suppress the duplicates in all three surfaces (since-feed, thread history, and USER.md digest) at once. Singleton iOS batches (len==1) are unaffected and need no change. Add a test in test_ios_chat.py that enqueues 2-3 iOS messages into one thread, drains a single coalesced batch, and asserts the since-feed/history contain exactly one assistant row.

detail:
The reviewer's facts check out and the runtime symptom genuinely persists. send_gate_confirmation (messaging.py:311-351) is typed -> None and returns None in every branch; the no-surface branch (messaging.py:336-344) only emits a logger.warning. The caller views.py:172 invokes it without inspecting any outcome and always returns HTTP 202 {status:'pending', expires_at} (views.py:181-188). So an iOS-only App Store user (DeviceToken, no telegram_chat_id/line_user_id, preferred_channel defaulting to 'telegram') with GWS connected who triggers a destructive gated action (gmail_delete/calendar_delete/drive_delete/task_delete/gmail_send) still creates a PendingAction, the container polls GatePollView, and after 5 minutes is_expired (models.py:80-81) flips it to EXPIRED with no actionable prompt and no in-band failure surfaced to the container or user. The defect named exactly this silent-expiry symptom, and at the runtime level it is NOT resolved -- only the server-side log message changed (misleading 'no Telegram chat_id' -> accurate no-surface warning) and a doomed Telegram send was avoided.

It is genuine but narrow, and the disposition is honest. The fix correctly does what it claims within its constraints: routes channel resolution through resolve_user_channel via a lazy import (no circular import), keeps Telegram/LINE delivery unchanged, and silently fixes the prior 'preferred=line but only telegram linked' misroute; the 4 regression tests pass legitimately (model defaults '' match the assertions). The disposition is explicitly marked 'partial' and accurately documents the residual, correctly attributing it to cluster C33's single-file scope (only apps/actions/messaging.py): surfacing a failure to the container requires views.py, and the APNs/since-feed/app-respond path requires apps/router. The disposition also argues -- reasonably -- against firing a bare APNs alert the user could not act on. So this is not a wrongly-dismissed defect, not a regression, and not a broken fix; it is an honestly-scoped partial whose user-visible defect (silent gate expiry for iOS-only GWS users) remains live. I rate it genuine-incomplete at LOW severity: the runtime impact is real but the blast radius is tightly bounded (iOS-only + GWS connected + destructive gated action), the original defect's interim guard was 'surface a clear failure' which is only partially met (server log, not container/user), and the residual is openly tracked for the follow-up apps/router work.
suggested_fix: To fully satisfy the interim guard within the actions app: have send_gate_confirmation return a boolean (delivered/undelivered) and have views.py inspect it -- when no usable channel exists, return a response that the container can act on (e.g. status 'undeliverable' or an immediate non-pending result with a reason), instead of HTTP 202 'pending' that only ends in a silent 'expired' poll. The complete fix (tracked in the disposition) adds, in apps/router, a gate push + ?since= gate row for channel=='app' plus an iOS-callable gate-respond endpoint wrapping GateRespondView, then wires send_gate_confirmation to invoke that app path when resolve_user_channel returns 'app'.

detail: The claim is technically accurate. apps/actions/tasks.py:25 builds PendingAction.objects.filter(status=PENDING, expires_at__lt=now()) with no select_related. PendingAction.tenant is a ForeignKey (actions/models.py:32) and Tenant.user is a OneToOneField (tenants/models.py:142), so both lazy-load on access. Per expired action the loop incurs two extra DB round-trips: (1) action.tenant at tasks.py:36 inside ActionAuditLog.objects.create(tenant=action.tenant,...) triggers a tenant fetch; (2) update_gate_message then calls editor(action.tenant, action) which reads tenant.user.telegram_chat_id (messaging.py:37) or tenant.user.line_user_id (messaging.py:161), triggering a user fetch. The action.tenant access inside update_gate_message reuses the instance cached by step 1, so it is ~2 extra queries per action when a platform channel is set, and only 1 when platform_channel is empty (update_gate_message returns early at messaging.py:355 before touching tenant.user). The sweep IS now live in production: TASK_MAP wires expire_stale_actions to expire_stale_pending_actions (apps/cron/views.py:91) on an every-5-minute QStash schedule (expire-stale-actions, */5 * * * * in register_system_crons.py), so unlike the old dead Celery code these queries actually execute. So the N+1 pattern is real. BUT severity is genuinely low and the reviewer rates it correctly: the queryset only contains pending gate actions that expired within the last few minutes (5-min default TTL via default_expires_at, models.py:25), so it is empty on most ticks and at most a handful of rows even when populated. A single tenant rarely has many gate actions expiring simultaneously. No correctness impact, just a few avoidable queries on a low-frequency sweep.
suggested_fix: Add .select_related("tenant__user") to the queryset at apps/actions/tasks.py:25. This JOINs the FK and OneToOne into the initial fetch, collapsing both per-action lazy loads into the single sweep query. The path tenant__user is valid (tenant is FK to tenants.Tenant; Tenant.user is a OneToOne). One-line change.

detail:
The claim is technically correct: the FA-0026 stale-RUNNING recovery is gated behind _check_limits passing, and a real idempotency-key collision occurs when the daily cap is hit while a stranded row exists.

Verified flow:
- scheduler.py:48 calls execute_automation(scheduled_for=automation.next_run_at), so for SCHEDULE runs reference_utc == next_run_at. A stranded RUNNING row from a prior hard-kill carries the same window-deterministic key 'schedule:<id>:<epoch(next_run_at)>' (services.py:240-242) because next_run_at was never advanced (the kill happened before _advance_schedule at line 491).
- _check_limits runs FIRST (line 401). _runs_today_count (lines 144-150) filters AutomationRun purely by scheduled_for in the local-day window with NO status filter, so the stranded RUNNING row counts toward the per-TENANT daily cap.
- If runs_today >= MAX_RUNS_PER_DAY (12, services.py:22) and trigger_source==SCHEDULE, the limit-fail branch does a bare AutomationRun.objects.create(...) (lines 406-417) with idempotency_key='schedule:<id>:<same epoch>'. idempotency_key is unique=True (models.py:70), so this raises IntegrityError BEFORE _advance_schedule at line 418. The create is NOT inside the later transaction.atomic block (line 432). run_due_automations catches the exception (scheduler.py:50-53), logs, increments errors, and continues WITHOUT advancing next_run_at. The automation stays due and throws on every subsequent tick; the stale-running recovery (lines 449-459) is never reached because _check_limits short-circuited first. tests_audit_C17.py exercises the recovery path only with empty run history (cap not hit), so this collision case is untested.

Two caveats that put this at low (not the claimed medium 'permanent wedge'):
1. It is NOT permanent. The stranded row's scheduled_for is a fixed instant; once the local day rolls over (or enough earlier runs age out of the local-day window per _day_window_for_timezone, lines 136-141), the stranded row no longer counts, the cap clears, _check_limits passes, and the stale-recovery branch fires and advances next_run_at. So it self-heals at local midnight rather than wedging forever.
2. The preconditions are narrow and co-occurring: a hard-kill must strand a RUNNING row AND the same tenant must already be at >=12 runs on that same local day. Both are uncommon; together they are rare.

User/operator impact: for an affected tenant on the affected day, the scheduler throws an IntegrityError + logs a traceback every tick (error-log spam) and the automation's recovery is blocked until local midnight, so that scheduled run is missed for the rest of the day. No data corruption, no cross-tenant impact, no poisoned transaction. Real but bounded and rare — a genuine incomplete-fix gap.
suggested_fix: Make the SCHEDULE limit-fail branch tolerant of an existing row with the same idempotency_key, and always advance the schedule. Either (a) replace the bare AutomationRun.objects.create(...) at services.py:406-417 with get_or_create on idempotency_key (wrapped in transaction.atomic), or (b) wrap the create in try/except IntegrityError. In both cases run _advance_schedule(automation, scheduled_for=reference_utc) and save next_run_at unconditionally even when the create collides, so a stranded row + cap-hit cannot block schedule advancement. Optionally exclude stranded/stale RUNNING rows (or non-terminal rows) from _runs_today_count so a wedged row does not consume cap headroom. Add a regression test mirroring tests_audit_C17 but seeding the tenant to MAX_RUNS_PER_DAY alongside a stranded RUNNING row, asserting next_run_at advances and no IntegrityError escapes.

detail:
Real, narrow gap in the FA-0026 fix. The fix added a stale-RUNNING re-dispatch self-heal, but it lives AFTER the limit check: execute_automation calls _check_limits at services.py:401 BEFORE get_or_create at :433. When the limit check returns not-allowed for a SCHEDULE trigger, control goes to the early SKIPPED branch (services.py:406-417) which does a bare AutomationRun.objects.create(...) with the window-deterministic key schedule:<id>:<epoch> (epoch derived from scheduled_for == automation.next_run_at). idempotency_key is unique=True (models.py:70), so if a stranded RUNNING row for that same window already exists, the create raises IntegrityError instead of short-circuiting into the new re-dispatch recovery (which only runs on the created=False path it never reaches). run_due_automations catches it as a generic errors+=1 (scheduler.py:50-53) and continues WITHOUT advancing next_run_at, so the automation is re-selected every tick and crashes forever — the exact unrecoverable, silent failure class the original defect described, now reachable through a different door.

The reviewer's MIN_RUN_INTERVAL trigger is valid and concrete: last_run_at is set (services.py:487) only on a SUCCESS, and only SCHEDULE successes advance next_run_at (services.py:490). A successful MANUAL run (views.py:113, no scheduled_for -> now) sets last_run_at to ~now (later than the stranded window T) WITHOUT advancing next_run_at, leaving it pinned at T. On the next scheduler tick, _check_limits(reference_utc=T) computes (T - last_run_at) as a negative timedelta, which is < 120min, so MIN_RUN_INTERVAL fails -> SKIPPED create -> IntegrityError collision with the stranded row's key.

Two caveats on framing: (1) the SKIPPED-branch create() was non-idempotent BEFORE the fix too, so the collision mechanism is pre-existing, not literally introduced by the fix; what the fix did was claim self-healing for strands while leaving this sub-path uncovered, so it is an INCOMPLETE fix rather than a pure regression. (2) Reachability is strictly rarer than the original FA-0026: it needs the same sub-ms hard-kill strand PLUS a subsequent last_run_at advance (e.g. a manual Run-now) while next_run_at stays pinned, PLUS the stranded row still present and not yet healed by a limit-passing tick. That compound precondition is why low (not medium) is right. The original defect's suggested reaper / advance-next_run_at-in-atomic-block fix would not have this hole.
suggested_fix: Make the limit-check-fail SKIPPED branch idempotent and strand-aware: replace the bare AutomationRun.objects.create at services.py:406 with get_or_create(idempotency_key=...) (defaults = the SKIPPED row) wrapped in transaction.atomic, and when created=False, apply the same stale-RUNNING re-dispatch / terminal-row handling used in the created=False block (or simply still advance next_run_at). Even simpler and more robust per the original suggested_fix: advance next_run_at unconditionally for SCHEDULE triggers inside the same atomic block as run creation (so a strand can never leave next_run_at pinned), and/or add the stale-RUNNING reaper cron. Either eliminates the deterministic-key collision crash loop entirely.

detail: Confirmed as a low-severity test-coverage gap, not a functional defect. The new receiver _bump_on_pending_extraction (apps/dashboard/receivers.py:96-103) is correct and complete: it delegates to the same _bump_for(instance) helper the four pre-existing receivers use, and the underlying fix (bumping the dashboard cache tag when ExtractionDismissView / typed-task-approve write only PendingExtraction.status) genuinely resolves the original FA-0173 stale-cache defect. However, apps/dashboard/test_receivers.py adds dedicated save/delete/status-change tests for all four pre-existing receivers (AssistantInsight, UserVoicePref, Goal, Document) and NONE for PendingExtraction: no PendingExtraction import and no test_pending_extraction_* method exist in that file. PendingExtraction is exercised in apps/journal/test_extraction.py and test_phase_integration.py, but never for the cache-tag-bump behavior. User impact today is nil (the receiver works); the real cost is future-regression risk: a rename of _bump_for, a broken import, or a refactor could silently disable this receiver with zero CI signal, re-introducing the exact stale-cache bug FA-0173 fixed. This is a genuine completeness/test-hygiene gap consistent with the reviewer's own low/incomplete framing. It does not undermine the functional fix; it leaves the new behavior the only uncovered receiver in an otherwise fully-tested file.

detail:
The claim is technically accurate and the scenario is reachable. The shipped fix (page.tsx:564-577) only widens kindFilter/relFilter when positions[id] is missing; it never lifts cluster isolation, so a neighbour hidden by isolation (not by kind/rel) still does not appear on canvas and the camera does not move.

Repro: tap a Cluster (line 516 -> setIsolated(cluster)+setSelectedId), then tap a member Lesson (line 517 -> selectedId=lesson). The Inspector's neighbors useMemo (line 580) and `selected` (line 562) both read UNFILTERED graphData.edges/nodes, so a cross-cluster SIMILAR_TO neighbour renders as a clickable Relationships button. Clicking it calls focusNode(crossClusterLessonId). That node is dropped from visibleNodes by isolation at line 417 (cluster_id !== isoCid), NOT by kindFilter, so it has no position entry -> focusNode takes the `if (!p)` branch. target.kind='Lesson' is already in kindFilter and its rels (SIMILAR_TO/IN_CLUSTER) already in relFilter, so setKindFilter (line 572) and setRelFilter (lines 573-574) are no-ops. `isolated` is never cleared, so visibleNodes still excludes the target -> positions still lacks it -> the node render early-returns null at line 690, and the pan/zoom branch (line 578) is skipped. No node appears, no camera move.

Important nuance that justifies LOW (vs the original MEDIUM): the fix is NOT a full no-op here. setSelectedId(id) runs, and because `selected`/`neighbors` read unfiltered data, the Inspector side panel re-renders for the clicked cross-cluster lesson (its title, body, relationships). So the user DOES get visible feedback (the panel switches) -- unlike the original 'absolutely nothing' grievance. The residual gap is purely the canvas: the node stays off-screen and the camera doesn't fly to it while isolation persists. So the original 'zero feedback' is genuinely narrowed, but a real residual UX rough edge remains (the camera/pan promised by focusNode's happy path still doesn't fire for isolation-hidden neighbours). Verified no other code path clears isolation on a neighbour-button click (only background tap at line 512, the isolation chip at line 649, Reset view at line 642, and toggleKind('Cluster') at line 560 do).
suggested_fix: In focusNode's `if (!p)` branch, also lift isolation when the target lies outside the isolated cluster: e.g. `if (isolated) setIsolated(null);` before (or alongside) the filter widening, so the next layout pass includes the target and the subsequent pan/zoom can run. Optionally, after lifting isolation the function could fall through to compute the position and pan/zoom on the next render, or the Inspector neighbors useMemo could annotate cross-isolation neighbours (visibleIds.has(other.id)===false) and dim/label them so the user knows the click will leave the isolated view.

detail:
Genuine, minor (cosmetic) regression introduced by the fix. Verified against the git diff of commit c1124d2 (the Phase-3 fix commit).

What the fix did: it renamed `isRevoked` (which matched only "revoked"/"error") to `needsReconnect` and added "expired", then reused that single predicate in THREE places — description, button label, AND the `badgeStatus` ternary (page.tsx:601-607). Pre-fix `badgeStatus` was `isActive ? "active" : isRevoked ? "error" : isConnected ? (integration?.status ?? "pending") : "pending"`. An "expired" record (isActive=false, isRevoked=false, isConnected=true) fell into the `isConnected` branch and rendered the raw status "expired", which StatusPill maps to AMBER (status-pill.tsx:23). Post-fix, "expired" is now caught by `needsReconnect` and `badgeStatus` becomes "error", which StatusPill maps to ROSE (status-pill.tsx:24). So the expired badge went amber → rose. The same collapse hits "revoked": pre-fix it rendered raw "revoked" → SLATE (status-pill.tsx:22); post-fix it becomes "error" → rose. Three distinct StatusPill tones (expired=amber, revoked=slate, error=rose) are now collapsed into one rose tone for the non-active states.

This contradicts the defect's own scope: the FA-0362 detail explicitly says "the StatusPill correctly shows 'expired' in amber" and the suggested_fix said only to change the line-587 reconnect predicate so the description/button match the badge — it did NOT ask to touch badge logic. The intended end state (per the defect) was description "Reconnection required" + button "Reconnect" while KEEPING the amber expired badge. The fix instead changed the badge too.

User impact: real but small. An expired/revoked integration now shows a rose "error" pill instead of its intended amber "expired" / slate "revoked" pill, losing the design system's deliberate visual distinction between "lapsed/needs-reconnect" (amber) and "hard error" (rose). No functional/data impact — description, button, and OAuth recovery flow all work correctly. Note the underlying defect (description/button mismatch) WAS correctly fixed; only the badge color is a side-effect regression. Severity low (cosmetic, design-token level).
suggested_fix: Don't reuse `needsReconnect` for the badge. Keep the badge driven by the raw status so StatusPill renders its per-status tone. E.g. set `badgeStatus = isActive ? "active" : isConnected ? (integration?.status ?? "pending") : "pending"` (drop the `needsReconnect ? "error"` arm entirely, since StatusPill already maps revoked→slate, expired→amber, error→rose). Leave the description and button predicates using `needsReconnect`. This restores the amber expired / slate revoked badges while preserving the FA-0362 description/button fix.

detail:
Genuine fix-introduced regression, confirmed by reading the patched file and the fix disposition. The FA-0427 fix changed ONLY line 79 to `if (!prefersReduced) rafRef.current = requestAnimationFrame(draw);` (correctly stopping the infinite RAF loop for reduced-motion users) and left the `onResize` handler (lines 88-91) untouched. That handler calls `resize()` then `initStars()` but never `draw()`. `resize()` (lines 39-40) assigns `canvas.width`/`canvas.height`, which per the HTML canvas spec resets the drawing surface to transparent black on every assignment. Post-fix, reduced-motion users have no RAF loop and no other repaint trigger, so after any window-resize event the decorative starfield canvas goes permanently blank. The original buggy code masked this because its always-running RAF loop repainted on the next frame after `initStars()`. The component renders on the public landing page (app/page.tsx, twice) and the onboarding shell, so the affected surfaces are user-facing.

Severity is genuinely low, not higher: the canvas is purely decorative (aria-hidden, pointer-events-none) — no functional breakage, no broken interaction, no data loss; only the reduced-motion cohort is affected; and only after a resize event (desktop window resize, orientation change, or even mobile URL-bar show/hide firing `resize`). Visible effect is the background star field disappearing for those users. The original infinite-loop fix was correct in isolation but incomplete: it broke the draw-once-on-resize contract that the old loop implicitly satisfied.
suggested_fix: In the `onResize` handler (lines 88-91), repaint after re-initialising stars for the reduced-motion path. Change it to: `const onResize = () => { resize(); initStars(); if (prefersReduced) draw(); };`. For non-reduced-motion users the running RAF loop already repaints, so the guard limits the extra call to exactly the cohort that needs it. Cleanup (cancelAnimationFrame at line 95) remains correct.

detail: Two-part finding, both halves verified against committed code. HALF 1 (count semantics) is FIXED, contradicting the stale disposition: apps/finance/snapshot.py:32-44 increments created_count only when _create_snapshot_for_tenant returns non-None (it returns None on the duplicate-skip and no-accounts-skip paths), and tests at apps/finance/tests.py:1437 and :1447 assert count==0. So fix_dispositions.json (action=partial, files_changed=[], 'reverted it to avoid a worse regression') is stale/wrong; the fix WAS applied. HALF 2 (automatic scheduling) is STILL genuinely unfixed: create_monthly_snapshots is the only writer of FinanceSnapshot (FinanceSnapshot.objects.create appears only at snapshot.py:80) and has NO production caller. There is no wrapper in apps/finance/tasks.py (only schedule_finance_welcome_task lives there), no TASK_MAP entry in apps/cron/views.py, and no entry in apps/cron/management/commands/register_system_crons.py. Grep for create_monthly_snapshots / finance.snapshot imports across apps/ (excluding tests) returns nothing. DISAMBIGUATION: a 'snapshot-gravity-weekly' system cron DOES exist (register_system_crons.py:78, TASK_MAP snapshot_gravity_weekly at views.py:193) but it writes a DIFFERENT model, PillarSnapshot, via apps.insights.tasks.snapshot_gravity_weekly_task (apps/insights/tasks.py:70). It does NOT cover FinanceSnapshot, so this feature stays dormant. IMPACT: low/latent. The /api/v1/finance/snapshots/ endpoint (FinanceSnapshotListView, finance/urls.py:37) is wired and works, but will always return an empty list in production because no FinanceSnapshot row is ever written; no crash, it degrades gracefully to empty. But the module's documented core promise ('Create a FinanceSnapshot for each finance-enabled tenant ... on the 1st of each month via QStash') is never fulfilled, so monthly debt/savings/net-worth history never accumulates.
suggested_fix: Wire create_monthly_snapshots into the cron pipeline, mirroring the existing weekly Gravity snapshot. (1) Add a thin task wrapper in apps/finance/tasks.py (e.g. create_monthly_snapshots_task) that imports and calls apps.finance.snapshot.create_monthly_snapshots(). (2) Register it in TASK_MAP in apps/cron/views.py (e.g. 'snapshot_finance_monthly': 'apps.finance.tasks.create_monthly_snapshots_task'). (3) Add a monthly schedule entry in apps/cron/management/commands/register_system_crons.py (cron '0 6 1 * *' -> /api/cron/trigger/snapshot_finance_monthly/). The function is already idempotent per (tenant, date) via the duplicate-skip in _create_snapshot_for_tenant, so re-fires are safe. Alternatively, if FinanceSnapshot is intended to be superseded by PillarSnapshot, delete the dead snapshot.py module, FinanceSnapshot model/migration, and FinanceSnapshotListView/url to avoid a permanently-empty endpoint.
