detail: The claim is structurally accurate and represents a genuine, narrow incompleteness — the same orphaned-proposed-topic class that FA-0596 fixed survives unfixed in the sibling call site. Verified in source: (1) `_record_insight_impl` (views.py:355-371) was correctly wrapped in `with _tx.atomic():` so resolve_topic's proposed-topic INSERT and the AssistantInsight.create commit or roll back together. (2) `_upsert_voice_pref_impl` (views.py:629-658) is NOT wrapped: it calls `resolve_topic` at line 644 — which is `@transaction.atomic` (topic_resolver.py:47) and, with no enclosing atomic block and no ATOMIC_REQUESTS in config, COMMITS the PROPOSED TopicRegistry row when it returns — then calls `UserVoicePref.objects.update_or_create` at line 652. If that downstream write fails, the proposed topic is already committed and orphaned (no voice pref references it), with no reaping cron. (3) FA-0617's fix touched only topic_resolver.py and addressed a DIFFERENT failure class — the concurrent-INSERT TOCTOU race (savepoint + IntegrityError catch-and-requery), not the orphan-on-downstream-failure pattern; its own summary lists `_upsert_voice_pref_impl` as a call site that "benefits without modification," but that benefit is only for the race, not for atomicity across resolve_topic+update_or_create. So FA-0596's disposition summary ("preventing orphaned proposed-topic rows") is true only for the record-insight path. Real-world impact is LOW and matches FA-0596's own rating: the trigger window is narrow (update_or_create itself upserts, so the unique_together IntegrityError is largely self-handled; the realistic trigger is a generic DB error like a connection drop in the inter-statement window), and the orphan is benign — proposed topics are expected registry churn and the orphan dangles harmlessly with no referencing row. Not a user-visible bug; a defense-in-depth / data-tidiness gap left asymmetric by the fix.
suggested_fix: Wrap the body of `_upsert_voice_pref_impl` (resolve_topic call through update_or_create) in `transaction.atomic()`, mirroring the FA-0596 fix in `_record_insight_impl`. resolve_topic's existing `@transaction.atomic` then degrades to a savepoint that rolls back the proposed-topic INSERT if update_or_create raises. Use the same function-local lazy `from django.db import transaction as _tx` import pattern used at views.py:358 to avoid the lint hook stripping an unused module-level import.

detail: Genuine. The fix at apps/journal/views.py:535 calls update_tenant_config_task.delay(str(tenant.id)), but update_tenant_config_task (apps/orchestrator/tasks.py:28) is a plain Python function with no Celery decorator and no .delay() attribute. The project explicitly removed Celery for QStash (config/settings/base.py:37 comment: django_celery_beat removed - using QStash for scheduling); there is no celery dependency, and the whole app contains exactly two .delay() call sites - these two journal/views.py calls. Everything else dispatches via publish_task('update_tenant_config', tenant_id), with that task name registered in TASK_MAP at apps/cron/views.py:99. At runtime, plain_function.delay raises AttributeError, swallowed by the surrounding except Exception: pass block. So the config push never fires for either the new DELETE path or the pre-existing PATCH path (lines 507-511). The fix disposition claims it ensures the agent container's templates.md is refreshed after delete-and-promote, eliminating the stale-config window - that behaviour is NOT delivered. update_tenant_config (apps/orchestrator/services.py:600-616) is what re-uploads templates.md (NBHD_SKILL_TEMPLATES_MD) to the running container; without it the container keeps the stale boot-time copy. User impact: a subscriber who deletes or edits their default note template via the web console sees the change in Django, but the running OpenClaw container serves the old daily-journal template slugs until the next unrelated config bump or reboot - a self-healing stale-config window, hence low severity. The fix faithfully copied an established-but-broken pattern, which is exactly the incomplete failure mode: a no-op call does not satisfy the defect's expected behaviour.
suggested_fix: In apps/journal/views.py replace both update_tenant_config_task.delay(str(tenant.id)) calls (the new DELETE path near line 535 and the pre-existing PATCH path near line 509) with the QStash dispatch used everywhere else: import publish_task from apps.cron.publish and call publish_task('update_tenant_config', str(tenant.id)). Keep the best-effort try/except. Optionally pass an idempotency_key derived from the tenant id to dedupe rapid edits.

detail: GENUINE incomplete fix. The FA-0742 fix in apps/journal/session_views.py:162-172 replaced the raw `since` filter with `parsed_since = parse_datetime(since) or parse_date(since)` and only handles the `parsed_since is None` branch (400). The disposition's explicit premise — that parse_datetime/parse_date 'both return None on bad input, never raise' — is FALSE. Verified directly against this repo's Django 5.1.15 (/Users/michaeljones/Projects/nbhd-united/.venv): parse_date('2021-13-45'/'2021-02-30'/'2021-04-31'/'9999-99-99') and parse_datetime('2021-13-45T10:00:00'/'2021-01-01T25:61:00'/'2021-02-30T10:00:00') all RAISE ValueError (only purely syntactically-malformed strings like 'not-a-date' return None). I also ran the exact line-166 expression `parse_datetime(s) or parse_date(s)` for s in '2021-13-45','2021-02-30','9999-99-99','2021-02-30T10:00:00' — every one raised ValueError, escaping line 166 with no try/except around it. config/settings/base.py:130 REST_FRAMEWORK has no EXCEPTION_HANDLER, so DRF's default handler (only converts Http404/PermissionDenied/APIException) does NOT catch ValueError → it propagates as HTTP 500. So the fix DID close the common `?since=not-a-date` case (now a clean 400), but an out-of-range family — `?since=2021-13-45`, `?since=2021-02-30`, `?since=9999-99-99`, `?since=<bad>T<bad>` — still returns the exact opaque 500 + noisy server error FA-0742 was meant to eliminate. No `since` test was added (apps/journal/tests_sessions.py has only test_list_invalid_limit_returns_400 at line 297), so the gap is unguarded against regression. User impact matches the original low severity: an authenticated API caller passing a plausible-but-out-of-range date still gets a 500 instead of an actionable 400; no data-integrity or security impact.
suggested_fix: Wrap the parse in try/except, mirroring the `limit` block 13 lines below (session_views.py:178-184). Replace lines 166-172 with: `try: parsed_since = parse_datetime(since) or parse_date(since)` / `except (ValueError, TypeError): parsed_since = None`, then keep the existing `if parsed_since is None: return Response({'detail': 'since must be an ISO date/datetime.'}, status=400)` and the filter. Add a test for `?since=2021-13-45` (and an in-range valid case) to tests_sessions.py to guard the regression.

detail:
The security fix itself is correct and complete - there is NO live vulnerability. SessionDetailView.get_permissions() (apps/journal/session_views.py:195-200) returns [HasSessionsWriteScope()] for DELETE and keeps HasSessionsReadScope for GET; HasPATScope.has_permission (apps/tenants/permissions.py:23-31) passes through for JWT (auth_pat is None) so the console is unaffected; DELETE stays tenant-filtered via get_object_or_404(..., tenant=tenant). A read-only PAT genuinely cannot delete sessions in the shipped code, so no real user/operator is exposed.

The claim is accurate ONLY as a test-coverage / regression-safety gap. The suggested_fix for FA-0744 explicitly required a regression test asserting a sessions:read-only PAT receives 403 on DELETE while a sessions:write PAT succeeds. That test was not added: the disposition files_changed is just ['apps/journal/session_views.py'] with no test file (fix_dispositions.json:667-673). The only DELETE test (apps/journal/tests_sessions.py:345 test_delete_session) runs under JWT auth (SessionDetailTest.setUp lines 320-325 uses RefreshToken), which bypasses scope checks entirely and never exercises the new write-scope guard. The PAT scope-test class (SessionScopeTest, lines 160-219) covers ingest/list/get-detail for read and write PATs but has NO DELETE case at all.

Net: this is not a user-facing defect - the privilege-escalation fix works. It is a genuine but minor incompleteness: the explicitly-required regression test guarding the just-fixed escalation path was omitted, so a future revert of DELETE to the read scope would pass the suite silently. Correctly rated low / incomplete.
suggested_fix: Add two tests to SessionScopeTest in apps/journal/tests_sessions.py (it already has _pat_with_scopes and self.session): (1) test_read_scope_cannot_delete - auth with a ['sessions:read'] PAT, DELETE /api/v1/sessions/{self.session.id}/, assert 403 and that the session still exists; (2) test_write_scope_can_delete - auth with a ['sessions:write'] PAT, DELETE the same endpoint, assert 204 and that the session is gone. This pins the get_permissions() override against regression.

detail:
The mechanism is real and verified against the code. create_connections (services.py:103-106) reconciles by deleting BOTH directions of 'similar' edges relative to the reconciling lesson's own current top-5 peer set. Its inbound clause Q(to_lesson=lesson) & ~Q(from_lesson_id__in=current_peer_ids) deletes Y->X edges (and paired X->Y) whenever Y is not in X's current top-5. Because the fix always writes both directions per peer (services.py:113-122) and find_similar_lessons caps at top-5 (limit=5, services.py:53), and cosine top-5 MEMBERSHIP is asymmetric even though similarity VALUE is symmetric (X may have 5 peers closer than Y, evicting Y from X's top-5 while X stays in Y's top-5), X's reconcile can authoritatively delete an edge that only Y's assessment created. The pre-fix accretion-only code (get_or_create only) never deleted these, so this IS a genuine NEW over-deletion the parent defect (which only asked to remove THIS lesson's stale OUTBOUND edges) did not request.

That said, the user/operator impact is effectively nil and self-healing, and I confirmed every bound the reviewer cites: (1) the dropped pair re-surfaces in BOTH constellation (views.py:148-179) and galaxy (views.py:226-256) as a recomputed 'affinity' edge, because any pair that was a stored 'similar' edge had similarity >= 0.75 (find_similar_lessons threshold, services.py:52), which always clears the affinity recompute floor (min_sim=0.3, or 0.1 for <=5 lessons) — so no edge vanishes from the rendered graph; (2) star-stage promotion via connections_out is monotonic/promote-only (growth.py:47-54), so a reduced count cannot demote; (3) the whole path is reachable only via the operator-run one-time rewrite_lessons_actionable command (plus reseed/backfill), not any user-facing edit endpoint — the same exposure ceiling the parent defect established. Net visible effect: a transient stored-'similar' -> recomputed-'affinity' edge-type drift (possibly a slightly different edge color/weight) for one pair until peer Y next reprocesses. No data loss, no demotion, no missing link. Genuine but purely cosmetic — same severity class as the parent defect, just a new behavioral wart rather than the one the fix targeted.
suggested_fix: Scope the inbound DELETE clause so the reconciling lesson is only authoritative over the edges IT authored, not over edges other lessons authored toward it. Two clean options: (a) Drop the inbound clause entirely and delete only outbound stale edges: filter(connection_type='similar', from_lesson=lesson).exclude(to_lesson_id__in=current_peer_ids).delete(), and rely on each peer's own reconcile to prune its outbound edges. This means a stale X->Y survives until X reprocesses, but it eliminates the cross-lesson over-deletion and keeps the symmetric-pair invariant looser. (b) Keep both-direction pruning but only delete a Y->X inbound edge when X is also absent from a recomputed check against Y (i.e. confirm the pair is non-similar from both sides before removing) — heavier, requires a per-edge similarity recheck. Given the impact is cosmetic and self-healing, option (a) is the proportionate fix; preserve the existing test_services idempotency assertion (created_again==0 when the similar set is unchanged) and keep the connection_type='similar'-only scoping so user-curated edges stay intact.

detail:
Confirmed real regression introduced by the FA-0913 audit fix. Commit c1124d2 added `raw_user_text = redact_user_message(raw_user_text, tenant)` at poller.py:1375, and that now-redacted text is passed as user_text_excerpt (poller.py:1435) and stored verbatim on PendingMessage.user_text (pending_queue.py:375). The dropped-message apology _send_apology_for_dropped_pending_message (pending_queue.py:1106-1159) reads msg.user_text, runs only strip_internal_framing — whose _INTERNAL_FRAMING_TAGS (error_messages.py:959-965) covers System/Now/chat/User tapped button/Photo attached but NOT [PERSON_N]/[EMAIL_ADDRESS_N] placeholders — and sends it to the user with no rehydrate_text call (Telegram httpx.post 1150-1153; LINE _send_line_text 1129). Every other outbound channel path DOES rehydrate (poller.py:331, pending_queue.py:1521/1568, cron_delivery.py:175, chat_views.py:435, siri_views.py:108); only these apology paths were missed. The new test tests_audit_C01.py:74-75 codifies the redacted excerpt at rest. Pre-fix the excerpt was the user's real text, so the apology quoted real words — a true behavioural regression. The claim is slightly understated: a SECOND identical leak path exists, _send_apology_for_stale_pending_message (pending_queue.py:1028), which reads msg.user_text as an excerpt with neither strip_internal_framing NOR rehydrate. User impact is real but bounded: the apology goes to the subscriber THEMSELVES (the owner of the PII), so there is no third-party privacy exposure — it is a cosmetic/UX defect where a user whose dropped/stale message contained a name/email/phone sees e.g. 'It started with: \"[EMAIL_ADDRESS_1] about it…\"' instead of their own words. It only fires on a rare error path (PII in the message AND a 3-attempt delivery failure or a too-stale queue). Hence low severity, not medium.</final_detail>
<suggested_fix>In both apology helpers, rehydrate the stored excerpt before quoting. At pending_queue.py:1112 wrap with rehydrate_for_tenant: `excerpt = strip_internal_framing(rehydrate_for_tenant(tenant, msg.user_text or '')).strip().replace('\n', ' ')`, and at pending_queue.py:1028 apply both rehydrate_for_tenant and strip_internal_framing similarly (the stale path is currently missing the framing strip too). Import `from apps.pii.redactor import rehydrate_for_tenant`. Update tests_audit_C01.py to assert the at-rest stored excerpt is redacted (current behaviour, correct for the no-PII-to-LLM goal) AND add a test that the apology TEXT the user receives is rehydrated back to real words.</suggested_fix>

detail: The claim is factually accurate end-to-end and is a real side effect introduced by the FA-0913/0914 fix. Verified chain: (1) the audit fix (commit c1124d28) added redact_user_message(raw_user_text, tenant) at poller.py:1375, and raw_user_text flows as user_text_excerpt (poller.py:1435) into PendingMessage.user_text (pending_queue.py:375), which join_user_texts(batch) reads redacted (pending_queue.py:1314). So Telegram ConversationTurn.user_text is now stored in placeholder space (e.g. '[PERSON_1] emailed me at [EMAIL_ADDRESS_1]'); pre-fix it was real text. (2) clean_reply_for_capture (conversation_capture.py:88-92) DOES rehydrate the reply to real names, stored in reply_text (pending_queue.py:1315). (3) build_conversation_digest renders both user and reply verbatim with no rehydration (conversation_capture.py:297-302). (4) That digest is injected into USER.md via apps/journal/envelope.py:274-276, auto-loaded into the container every agent turn and cron. (5) iOS AppChatMessage.user_text is stored raw (chat_views.py:190/205; comment 218-219 keeps the user's own text raw), so the digest mixes redacted Telegram user-lines + rehydrated Telegram reply-lines + raw iOS lines. The asymmetric history is real and a behavior change vs pre-fix. I rate it LOW (not the claimed medium): impact is minor because (a) it fails SAFE - the regression makes the digest LESS revealing (placeholders), never a PII leak; (b) the per-tenant container already operates in [PERSON_1] placeholder space for live Telegram inbound, so a placeholder user-line is consistent with what the agent already sees - it is the rehydrated reply-line and raw iOS line that are the odd ones out, not the user-line; (c) the digest is a soft proactive-grounding hint, not a correctness-critical surface, so partial placeholdering slightly hurts readability/grounding but breaks no behavior. Note the parallel iOS context endpoint (ChatContextView, chat_views.py:430-437) already rehydrates the whole digest at render time before serving - the USER.md envelope path simply does not, which is the gap.
suggested_fix: Make the digest symmetric. Cleanest option mirrors what chat_views.py:430-437 already does for the iOS context endpoint: in build_conversation_digest (conversation_capture.py), rehydrate the final assembled digest string via apps.pii.redactor.rehydrate_text(text, tenant.pii_entity_map) before returning (fail-open on error), so user-lines, reply-lines, and iOS lines all render in real-value space consistently. Alternatively, stop redacting the at-rest user_text_excerpt (revert poller.py:1375 for the persisted excerpt only) - but that re-introduces raw PII at rest, so the render-time rehydrate is preferable. Either way add a small test asserting a Telegram ConversationTurn with a placeholder user_text renders the real name in the digest.

detail: Verified genuine but low-impact. The early audio gate (line_webhook.py:937-940) rejects voice before the paid Whisper call only when `not _audio_tenant.onboarding_complete or _audio_tenant.onboarding_step == 0`. The downstream gate (lines 1114-1125) additionally runs needs_reintroduction(tenant) (onboarding.py:571-587), which returns True for onboarding_complete=True users whose profile is mostly defaults (>=3 of: display_name in {Friend, empty}, timezone in {UTC, empty}, language in {en, empty}, no onboarding_interests), then RESETS onboarding_step to 0 and rejects any non-text message. Confirmed a completed-onboarding user ends at onboarding_step=5 (onboarding.py:487-488), and the module docstring (onboarding.py:9-10) explicitly notes backfilled users sit at onboarding_complete=True with onboarding_step=4 and no real data. For such a re-introduction-eligible user sending voice: the early gate evaluates `not True (False) or step==0 (False, step is 4/5)` => False => NOT rejected early => _show_loading + _transcribe_line_audio (paid OpenAI whisper-1 call, line 53/146-150) run => then the downstream needs_reintroduction branch resets step=0 and rejects with 'please type your answer.' That is the exact waste (long loading animation + discarded paid transcript before an outright rejection) the early gate was added to eliminate. The fix's own comment ('needs_reintroduction reset still runs below for non-onboarding users') glosses over the fact that those users are ALSO re-onboarded and rejected, so the early gate is genuinely incomplete. Scope is bounded: LINE-only, only the backfilled/default-profile cohort, only when they send VOICE specifically, and behavior stays CORRECT (they are still rejected) -- only the cost optimization (one Whisper call plus a loading animation per such voice message) leaks. No correctness, data, or security impact. Memory notes ~17/27 tenants on UTC default, so the cohort is non-trivial, but only the LINE-voice subset is hit -- hence low severity.
suggested_fix: Make the early gate mirror the downstream condition by also checking needs_reintroduction. E.g. replace the condition at line_webhook.py:938-940 with `if _audio_tenant is not None and (not _audio_tenant.onboarding_complete or _audio_tenant.onboarding_step == 0 or needs_reintroduction(_audio_tenant)):` (importing needs_reintroduction at the top of the audio branch). This pre-empts the paid Whisper call + loading animation for the re-introduction cohort exactly as it does for in-flight onboarding users. The downstream reset/rejection can stay as the authoritative path.

detail:
The narrow technical core is real, but the claimed impact is materially overstated. The FA-0913/0914 fix wired redaction into _forward_to_container at poller.py:1374-1375. When a caller omits raw_user_text, line 1353 binds raw_user_text = message_text (same original string). Line 1374 rebinds message_text to its redacted result, but raw_user_text still references the ORIGINAL pre-redaction string, so line 1375 runs redact_user_message a SECOND time over identical content. Each call triggers a fresh DeBERTa NER inference pass (_detect_pii): the pipeline is loaded once via cached get_pii_pipeline(), but each pii_pipeline(text) call is fresh with no per-text memoization, so the redundant inference is genuine.

BUT it only occurs on the two MINORITY paths that omit raw_user_text: poller.py:513 (_delayed_forward after container restart) and poller.py:821 (forwarding a pending message after a 'don't update' callback). The DOMINANT inbound path, a normal Telegram message at poller.py:993, explicitly passes raw_user_text captured BEFORE decoration (line 965), so the two strings differ: message_text carries the photo prefix + _build_session_context goals/tasks/journal block + original, while raw_user_text is the bare original. On that path the two redact calls do distinct, necessary work, NOT double work. So the claim's 'roughly doubling redaction latency on the inbound hot path' and 'wasteful on every Telegram turn' are wrong, because the redundancy never hits the common turn. Redacting the large session-context block (1374) is the whole point of FA-0913/0914 (text sent to the third-party LLM must be redacted) and is necessary, not waste; the second call on the affected paths runs over the small bare excerpt anyway.

No correctness consequence (the reviewer concedes this): placeholders are reused from the tenant map, not re-minted, so no counter inflation, and redaction is fail-open. Net: a real but very low-impact inefficiency confined to two non-dominant paths, which a real operator would not notice. Genuine only on its narrow core, with the inflated impact corrected.
suggested_fix: In _forward_to_container, avoid the second NER pass when raw_user_text is the same object as message_text. Capture the original message_text before line 1374, then: if raw_user_text is the pre-redaction message_text, reuse the redacted message_text for raw_user_text instead of calling redact_user_message again. Concretely: detect aliasing before redacting (e.g. `_alias = raw_user_text is message_text` at line 1353-ish, or capture `_orig = message_text`), redact message_text at 1374, then `raw_user_text = message_text if _alias else redact_user_message(raw_user_text, tenant)`. detail:
The code reads exactly as claimed. In relay_ai_response_to_telegram (pending_queue.py): line 1647 strips [[button:...]] markers from text, lines 1650-1652 build reply_markup, then lines 1654-1655 'if not text: return True' execute BEFORE the only send call at line 1657 (_send_telegram_markdown(chat_id, text, reply_markup=...)). So when the agent emits a buttons-ONLY reply (e.g. just [[button:Yes|confirm]][[button:No|deny]] with no prose), text becomes empty after stripping, the function returns True, and reply_markup is silently dropped — no buttons are ever sent. This is a real, reachable gap.

Two qualifiers bound the impact. (1) It is NOT a regression. The poller's pre-existing _send_rich_response has the byte-identical gate (poller.py:416 'if clean_text:' wraps its only send and likewise discards reply_markup when clean_text is empty), so the FA-1036 fix faithfully reproduced long-standing production behavior and reached parity with the poller — exactly what the fix set out to do. (2) Buttons-only replies are an edge case the agent is steered away from: telegram-formatting.md says buttons appear 'directly under your message', and cron-management.md plus the formatting docs all show prose/a question FOLLOWED by buttons. In the normal prose+buttons case the fix works correctly (markers stripped, buttons tappable via the existing agent:-prefixed callback receiver), so the fix's primary goals (stop literal marker leakage; make buttons tappable) ARE delivered for the common case. Even in the buttons-only case the markers no longer leak as literal text (text is empty, not the raw markers); the net effect of the gap is silence (nothing sent) on a rare output shape, not garbage. Genuine but low-severity, and a faithful pre-existing-parity gap rather than a new break.
suggested_fix:
In relay_ai_response_to_telegram, deliver the keyboard even when text is empty. Change lines 1654-1657 so that when buttons exist but text is empty, a message still goes out carrying reply_markup — e.g. `if not text: 
    if reply_markup: 
        return _send_telegram_markdown(chat_id, "​", reply_markup=reply_markup)  # zero-width or a minimal prompt 
    return True`. For full coverage, apply the same fix to the mirrored poller path (poller.py:416 `if clean_text:`) so both delivery routes send a buttons-only reply. Verify the send helper / Telegram accepts a message whose text is a single zero-width/placeholder char with reply_markup (Telegram requires non-empty text on sendMessage, so a minimal placeholder is needed rather than an empty string).

detail: Verified. All status-mutating paths do an unguarded read-check-write with no select_for_update / transaction.atomic, and no ATOMIC_REQUESTS in config/ (grep empty): Telegram poller (apps/router/poller.py:1586-1614), LINE postback (apps/router/line_webhook.py:1411-1432), GateRespondView (apps/actions/views.py:279-318), the GatePollView poll path itself (apps/actions/views.py:204-223), and the 60s sweep (apps/actions/tasks.py:25-33). Each reads the row, checks is_expired (models.py:81), then save(update_fields=[...]) with last-writer-wins. The agent container does NOT act on the APPROVED row directly -- it learns the resolution by polling GatePollView (views.py:191-242), which returns action.status. So if a user taps Approve at the deadline boundary (handler reads row PENDING and not-expired, writes APPROVED) while the sweep tick reads the same row PENDING and now>expires_at and its EXPIRED write lands last, the container's next poll returns EXPIRED and the destructive action the user explicitly approved silently never runs; two ActionAuditLog rows (APPROVED + EXPIRED) and a stomped gate message also result. The window is genuinely sub-second around the 5-minute deadline on a coinciding sweep tick, and the failure is fail-safe (the approved action simply does not run; nothing denied/expired is ever executed), so 'low' severity is correct. The claim is accurate and not over-stated.
suggested_fix: Make each resolution atomic and conditional so the button handler and the sweep serialize on the row instead of clobbering. Wrap each read-check-write (poller, line_webhook, GateRespondView, and the sweep loop in tasks.py) in transaction.atomic() with PendingAction.objects.select_for_update().get(...), re-checking status==PENDING and is_expired under the lock. Or use a single conditional UPDATE -- PendingAction.objects.filter(id=..., status=PENDING).update(status=APPROVED) -- and proceed only when rowcount==1, so an approve cannot overwrite an EXPIRED row and the sweep cannot overwrite an APPROVED one.

detail:
The code description is accurate. apps/byo_models/services.py:143-145 does a non-atomic read-modify-write: it reads cred.seed_version (the in-memory value from update_or_create -- no row lock / select_for_update) and writes cred.seed_version + 1. Two concurrent transactions for the same tenant/provider could both read N and write N+1, losing one bump. The atomic form is .update(seed_version=F('seed_version') + 1); since the row is already loaded inside the same transaction.atomic() block, switching to F() is a one-line, zero-risk hardening. That said, the user/operator impact is effectively nil and the reviewer was honest (severity low, 'Low today'): (1) This is a single-user, IsAuthenticated, tenant-scoped POST (BYOCredentialListView.post, views.py:74-98) -- only the tenant's own session pastes their own credential, so a same-tenant/same-provider concurrent double-write requires one human firing two paste requests in the same millisecond; not attacker-reachable or multi-actor. (2) seed_version is unused today: both the model help_text (models.py:52-60) and the inline comment (services.py:141-142) say a Phase 2 Codex entrypoint WILL compare it against an on-disk marker (future tense), and 'Phase 1 (Anthropic env-var) ignores this field.' So today a lost increment has zero functional consequence. (3) Even in the speculative Phase 2 the harm self-heals: every re-paste also rewrites the KV secret (_write_secret_to_kv runs before the row update) and resets status to PENDING, and the next paste bumps again. Net: a genuine but trivial non-atomic-increment nit worth fixing prophylactically before Phase 2 ships, not a defect a user/operator would realistically hit.</final_detail>
<parameter name="suggested_fix">Use an atomic DB-side increment instead of read-modify-write: BYOCredential.objects.filter(pk=cred.pk).update(seed_version=F("seed_version") + 1), importing F from django.db.models. After that, drop the cred.refresh_from_db() only if seed_version isn't needed on the returned object (it currently is, so keep refresh_from_db). This removes the lost-update window entirely with no behavioral change.

detail: Confirmed real gap. In ingest_healthkit_payload (apps/fuel/healthkit.py:582-666), the local tombstones set is loaded from the profile at line 589, updated with ALL deleted_external_ids at line 602 (tombstones.update(deleted_ids)), and used for in-batch dedup at line 627 -- but it is NEVER written back to FuelProfile.healthkit_tombstones. The only persistence path is the post_delete signal workout_deleted_record_healthkit_tombstone (apps/fuel/signals.py:132-158), which fires ONLY when row.delete() actually deletes a row. The delete loop (lines 599-601) only deletes rows with source=HEALTHKIT and a matching external_id. So when a deleted_external_ids entry has no matching deletable row, no row is deleted, no signal fires, and the tombstone is not persisted across requests. Trigger set (narrow but real): the HK sample was deleted in Apple Health before it ever synced; a double-delete (row already removed on a prior sync); or the row was otherwise already gone. In those cases the tombstone is lost, and a later anchor reset / app reinstall / 30-day re-backfill re-imports the same external_id, because neither the existing set (the row is gone) nor the lost tombstone blocks it. This contradicts the module's own invariant (docstring lines 9-13: deleted imports are tombstoned so a client anchor reset cannot resurrect them) and is the PR #847 resurrection class. Two scoping corrections that NARROW the reviewer's framing: (1) The adopted-row case is NOT actually vulnerable -- when an HK sample was adopted into a planned/manual session (line 312/431 stamps the HK external_id, source stays user/assistant), the delete loop skips it, but on re-import the existing query at lines 610-615 filters external_id WITHOUT source, finds the stamped row, and returns duplicate at line 627. So adopted rows are protected by existing, not the tombstone; the only true resurrection is when NO row of any source survives. (2) It needs a two-step sequence (delete-without-deletable-row, THEN anchor reset/reinstall), so it is an edge case. Hence severity low rather than medium. User impact when hit: a workout the user deleted in Apple Health silently reappears in their workout history/calendar and re-inflates volume/frequency/adherence aggregates. The existing test (tests.py:4095) only covers the row-exists delete path, so this gap is uncovered.
suggested_fix: In ingest_healthkit_payload, after the delete loop, persist the full tombstone set whenever deleted_ids is non-empty, independent of whether rows were actually deleted. E.g. after line 602, if profile is not None: keep the last _TOMBSTONE_CAP entries and profile.save(update_fields=["healthkit_tombstones","updated_at"]). Reuse the _TOMBSTONE_CAP cap from signals.py to stay consistent. Add a regression test covering a deleted_external_ids entry with no matching DB row, then a re-import of that external_id asserting status==duplicate after an anchor reset. (The post_delete signal can remain as the NBHD-side-delete path.)

detail: The structural claim is accurate and verified. apps/fuel/views.py WorkoutPlanListView.post (1190-1206) creates the WorkoutPlan via serializer.save() then calls _expand_plan_workouts() with (a) NO transaction.atomic(), (b) NO idempotency dedup, and (c) the raw plan.schedule_json -- skipping the runtime path's _validate_normalize_schedule. The runtime twin RuntimeWorkoutPlanListCreateView.post (runtime_views.py:1256-1299) was deliberately hardened for exactly these failures: same name+start_date+ACTIVE retry short-circuits (return 200 deduped), create+expand wrapped in one transaction.atomic() with an explicit comment that a mid-loop failure must roll back the plan row, and the schedule is validated/normalized first. The failure mode is real: _expand_plan_workouts does workout_def.get('category', ...) at runtime_views.py:1073, so any schedule_json day value that is not a dict (string/null) raises AttributeError partway through the loop, leaving the plan row plus a partial set of PLANNED Workout/PlanSlot rows committed with no atomicity to undo them. WorkoutPlanSerializer has no validate_schedule_json (validate_detail_json/validate_rpe/validate belong to WorkoutSerializer), so malformed schedules pass DRF validation untouched. With no dedup, a double-submit creates a second full duplicate calendar; the PATCH regen path (1244-1254) does the same unvalidated DELETE-future-PLANNED + re-expand with no atomicity. Context that lowers severity below the claimed 'medium': the reviewer's headline trigger -- a web-UI double-tap -- is NOT currently reachable. /fuel/plans/ is wired at urls.py:118 and is IsAuthenticated, but the Next.js frontend never POSTs to it (no createWorkoutPlan / fuel/plans POST anywhere in frontend/app|lib|components, only finance payoff-plans); product plan creation flows through the hardened runtime/assistant path. So this is a genuine LATENT divergence: the runtime view was explicitly hardened and its IsAuthenticated console twin was left unguarded, inheriting partial-plan and duplicate-calendar risk for any non-frontend caller (iOS app, direct API, future console UI, integration scripts). Real but currently low-blast-radius defect-in-waiting, not an actively-firing user-facing bug.
suggested_fix: Bring WorkoutPlanListView.post/patch to parity with the runtime path: (1) wrap the plan create + _expand_plan_workouts (and the PATCH DELETE+re-expand) in a single transaction.atomic() so a mid-loop failure rolls back the plan row and any partial PLANNED rows; (2) run schedule_json through _validate_normalize_schedule (or add a validate_schedule_json on WorkoutPlanSerializer) before expansion so non-dict day values are rejected with 400 instead of raising AttributeError mid-loop; (3) add the same name+start_date+ACTIVE idempotency short-circuit (return 200 deduped) to guard against double-submit duplicating the calendar. Ideally factor the runtime view's hardened create/regen logic into a shared helper both views call.

detail:
Confirmed at the code level. In apps/fuel/serializers.py, original_workout is in WorkoutSerializer.fields (line 83) but NOT in read_only_fields (lines 101-111). The model field (apps/fuel/models.py:212-219) is a ForeignKey('self', related_name='reschedules'); the Workout model has no custom manager (no objects= override), so DRF auto-generates a writable PrimaryKeyRelatedField backed by the global Workout._default_manager.all() queryset, which spans all tenants with no per-request scoping. Both write paths bind raw request.data through this serializer with no field filtering: WorkoutListView.post (views.py:307) and WorkoutDetailView.patch (views.py:342-347). The serializer validate()/create() only stamp tenant on the row itself; they never check that original_workout.tenant == request.user.tenant. RLS is not a backstop here -- per project memory the Django connection role bypasses RLS in prod (relock is effectively a no-op). So an authenticated tenant CAN (a) persist a cross-tenant FK (their own workout's original_workout pointing at another tenant's workout) and (b) use the endpoint as an existence oracle: a valid other-tenant workout UUID validates (201/200) while a non-existent UUID returns 400. This is a real tenant-isolation boundary violation, so the finding is genuine.

But the real-world impact is genuinely minimal, matching low severity. A grep across apps/ (excluding tests) shows original_workout/reschedules referenced ONLY in the model definition, its migration, and the serializer fields list -- it is never read back, never traversed cross-tenant, never used in any business logic, and nothing (not even server-side reschedule code) ever WRITES it. It is currently an inert, write-but-never-consumed field, so there is no data exfiltration beyond the API echoing back the bare UUID the caller already supplied. The oracle is also weak: workout PKs are 122-bit random UUIDv4 (models.py:169 default=uuid.uuid4), so they cannot be enumerated or guessed -- the oracle only confirms that a UUID the attacker already possesses belongs to some workout. No cross-tenant content leaks. Net: a legitimate latent isolation gap worth closing, but not an exploitable data-disclosure path today.
suggested_fix: Add "original_workout" to WorkoutSerializer.read_only_fields (apps/fuel/serializers.py:101-111). Nothing in the codebase sets this field via the API, so making it read-only breaks no current behavior and removes both the cross-tenant write and the existence-oracle. If reschedule linkage is ever needed via API later, replace the auto-generated field with an explicit PrimaryKeyRelatedField whose get_queryset() filters Workout.objects.filter(tenant=self.context["tenant"]).

detail:
The code is exactly as cited and the defect is real. RuntimeDailyNoteAppendView.post (apps/integrations/runtime_views.py:1287-1289) and RuntimeDocumentAppendView.post (2322-2323) both do an unguarded read-modify-write: get_or_create loads the full doc.markdown, the new entry is concatenated in Python, and doc.save() rewrites the whole field — with no transaction.atomic, no select_for_update, and no DB-side append. A grep confirms zero locking in either the integrations runtime path or the parallel apps/journal/document_views.py:418-419 DocumentAppendView, and Document.markdown (apps/journal/models.py:184) is a plain TextField with no version/optimistic-lock column. The model docstring even states "Both human and agent append to it," so concurrent writers is an expected pattern — the lost-update window is real: if two appends to the same daily note overlap, the second save() clobbers the first's appended entry, silently, with no error surfaced to the user.

The reviewer's concurrency premise largely holds. The per-tenant serialization that does exist (apps/router/pending_queue.py) is keyed by (tenant, channel, channel_user_id) and covers only INBOUND webhook messages — it does not serialize across channels (e.g. iOS app channel vs Telegram) and does not cover cron-triggered turns, which fire inside the container on OpenClaw's own scheduler (crons are registered via cron.add in apps/cron/gateway_client.py, not dispatched as Django turns). So two independent execution contexts (a cron-fired section write + a live chat quick-log append, or two distinct-channel turns) genuinely can arrive as overlapping HTTP appends to the same Document. The billing counters' F() usage (apps/billing/services.py) is a valid in-repo contrast showing the team already knows the safe pattern.

What holds severity to LOW rather than MEDIUM: the RMW window is one short DB round-trip (read row, write row in the same request), and it only loses data when two distinct writers hit the SAME daily-note row inside that narrow window. With the fleet at ~1 active tenant and appends being infrequent, the overlap is rare in practice — but it is a latent correctness bug that grows with scale and channel count, and silent data loss is the worst failure mode, so it is worth fixing.</final_detail>
<suggested_fix>Wrap each append in a transaction and re-read the row under a row lock so the read-modify-write is serialized at the DB. Minimal version: in both RuntimeDailyNoteAppendView.post and RuntimeDocumentAppendView.post, after get_or_create, do `with transaction.atomic(): doc = Document.objects.select_for_update().get(pk=doc.pk); <re-read doc.markdown>; doc.markdown = ...; doc.save(update_fields=["markdown", "updated_at"])`. Apply the same to apps/journal/document_views.py:418-419 DocumentAppendView for parity. (A pure F()-concat won't work cleanly here because the entry depends on rstrip() of the current value, so select_for_update is the right tool, mirroring the pending_queue locking pattern already used elsewhere in the repo.)</suggested_fix>

</invoke>

detail:
The code-level claim is ACCURATE but the impact is OVERSTATED: this is a latent inconsistency/footgun, not a live data-integrity bug any current user can hit.

Accurate part: In ExtractionApproveView.post (apps/journal/views.py:570-579), the LESSON branch calls _approve_lesson(pending) and discards the returned lesson_id; the save() omits 'lesson_id', so pending.lesson_id stays NULL. The three sibling approve paths all persist it: Telegram (apps/router/extraction_callbacks.py:304-306), LINE (apps/router/line_webhook.py:1531-1533), cron (apps/journal/extraction.py:674-677). _undo_lesson (extraction_callbacks.py:176-179) is a no-op when lesson_id is NULL, and _approve_goal/_approve_task self-persist their FK internally so their undo links survive the same discard. So the web path is genuinely the odd one out.

Why impact is overstated (the orphaned-lesson scenario is unreachable today): (1) The web approve UI cannot receive a LESSON. Its only data source is the dashboard/horizons pending_extractions list (apps/dashboard/views.py:252-256) which filters kind__in=[GOAL, TASK]; lessons are excluded, and the frontend (frontend/components/pending-goal.tsx on the horizons page) only POSTs approve for goal/task ids surfaced there. (2) No production path creates a PENDING-status LESSON: all three PendingExtraction creation sites in extraction.py (663/690/713) create status=APPROVED rows directly (auto-add immediately), and the auto-resolve signals in signals.py only handle GOAL/TASK; ExtractionApproveView only acts on status=PENDING rows, so its lesson branch has no real rows to act on. (3) There is no web/HTTP undo endpoint at all; _undo_lesson is reachable only from Telegram (extraction_callbacks.py:255) and LINE postback handlers (an inline button on a chat message), which a web-approved row would never have.

Net: a real, isolated one-line omission that is inconsistent with three sibling paths and would immediately bite if lessons were ever exposed to web approval, but it causes no user-visible harm in the current code. Worth fixing for consistency/defense, hence genuine at low severity.
suggested_fix: Mirror the sibling paths: capture and persist the return value in ExtractionApproveView's lesson branch. e.g. `lesson_id = None; ... elif pending.kind == LESSON: _, lesson_id = _approve_lesson(pending)`, then after setting status/resolved_at, `if pending.kind == LESSON and lesson_id: pending.lesson_id = lesson_id; pending.save(update_fields=["status","resolved_at","lesson_id"]) else: pending.save(update_fields=["status","resolved_at"])`. Optionally also drop the LESSON branch from ExtractionApproveView entirely if web lesson-approval is never intended (it is currently dead for real data).

detail:
Confirmed real defect, but lower impact than claimed. DocumentDetailView.get is wrapped in @tenant_cache(ttl=60, tag="journal") (apps/journal/document_views.py:296). For flag-enabled tenants the GET replaces doc.markdown with a live synthesis of typed Task/Goal rows (lines 318-322), and that synthesized response.data is cached for 60s under the 'journal' tag (cache.set in apps/common/cache.py:141-145). The 'journal' tag is bumped ONLY by post_save/post_delete receivers on JournalEntry, Document, and DailyNote (apps/common/cache_signals.py:91-104). There are NO receivers for Task or Goal, and grep confirms apps/journal/, apps/router/extraction_callbacks.py, and apps/integrations/runtime_views.py never call bump_tag/bump_tags. Every typed write path -- Task.complete()/Goal.mark_achieved()/abandon() (models.py:336-345,416-429), the web lifecycle views (lifecycle_views.py), and the typed approval creates -- does a plain save()/create() that bumps nothing. Net: after completing/creating/reopening a typed task or goal, GET /api/v1/journal/documents/{tasks|goal}/... serves the previously-cached synthesized snapshot for up to 60s, re-introducing the stale-snapshot failure at the HTTP cache layer.

Severity is LOW not medium because: (1) canary-gated (Tenant.experimental_typed_journal_lifecycle default=False, tenants/models.py:430) -- blast radius today is the canary tenant(s) only; (2) the staleness is transient and self-healing -- the 60s TTL expires and the next read is fresh, so it is a brief inconsistency window, not durable corruption; (3) it is confined to the legacy Document(kind=tasks|goal) markdown view. The typed collection/detail endpoints the iOS client and web UI use to enumerate tasks/goals -- TaskListCreateView/GoalListCreateView GET (lifecycle_views.py:197,238) and Task/GoalDetailView GET -- are NOT decorated with @tenant_cache, so they always render fresh. The latent defect does widen as the rollout broadens, so it is worth fixing before the flag goes fleet-wide.
suggested_fix: Add post_save/post_delete receivers for Task and Goal in apps/common/cache_signals.py:_register() that bump the 'journal' tag (and 'dashboard' if task/goal state feeds the dashboard), e.g. import Task, Goal from apps.journal.models and register a receiver calling _bump(instance, ["journal", "dashboard"]). This is the centralized, single-place pattern the module already enforces, so it covers all write paths (web lifecycle views, model methods, typed approval creates, agent runtime) without touching each call site. Tasks/Goals carry a tenant FK so _tenant_id() resolves correctly.

detail:
The structural defect is real but the claimed impact is overstated. In deprovision_tenant (apps/orchestrator/services.py:691-770) the OpenRouter sub-key and KV secret are deleted FIRST (lines 705-733), each inside its own log-and-continue try/except so those failures never reach the outer handler. The outer except (766-770) is reachable only when container/file-share/identity teardown (736-743) raises; it sets status=SUSPENDED and re-raises but does NOT clear openrouter_key_hash / openrouter_key_secret_name (nor container_id) — those fields are cleared only on the success path (750-762). So a partial deprovision can leave a SUSPENDED tenant whose key_hash/secret_name still reference an OR sub-key + KV secret that were already deleted, and on resubscribe handle_checkout_completed (apps/billing/services.py:333-337) takes the SUSPENDED->ACTIVE wake path (should_provision=False, no key re-mint), so the stale references persist into the live tenant. The except-path field-clearing gap is genuine.

Two corrections to the impact narrative: (1) The billing/credit path that 'trusts' the hash — sync_or_key_limit (apps/billing/credits.py:42-76) — is gated on OPENROUTER_PER_TENANT_KEYS_ENABLED (default False, config/settings/base.py:450) and is explicitly best-effort: update_sub_key on the dead hash throws but is swallowed by the try/except at line 75 with only a warning. It does NOT silently break a chat turn; check_budget + record_usage remain the real enforcement. So 'billing/credit logic operates on a key that no longer exists' is technically true but harmless (logged no-op). (2) The dangling OR reference is a minor facet of a deeper, unnamed defect on the SAME path: container_id is also left set on a container that delete_container_app already destroyed, so reactivation's scale_container_app (services.py:361-366) targets a deleted container — a 'partial-deprovision-marked-SUSPENDED is corrupt and not cleanly resumable' problem that dominates the OR-key symptom.

Reachability is narrow: requires per-tenant OR keys enabled in prod AND an Azure teardown failure that lands after the (best-effort) OR/KV deletes but during container/identity teardown AND a later resubscribe of that same tenant. Real but rare, and the dominant failure on resume would be the deleted-container wake, not the OR key. Net: genuine low-severity data-integrity/consistency gap, not the medium silent-inference-failure described.
suggested_fix: In the except block (services.py:766-770), since the OR sub-key + KV secret are unconditionally deleted before any teardown that can raise, clear openrouter_key_hash and openrouter_key_secret_name (and ideally container_id/container_fqdn/managed_identity_id if their delete already succeeded) when marking the tenant SUSPENDED — or, more robustly, perform the field clears immediately after each successful resource delete rather than batched at the end, so partial-failure state always matches reality. Better still: do NOT revert a partially-torn-down tenant to SUSPENDED (a resumable state) — use a distinct DEPROVISION_FAILED/needs-attention status so a half-deleted tenant can't be silently woken on resubscribe.

detail:
The claim's code logic is accurate and confirmed end-to-end. _stale_provisioning_tenants_queryset (services.py:89-97) deliberately includes Tenant.Status.ACTIVE in the repair candidate set, filtered to rows missing container_id OR container_fqdn. repair_stale_tenant_provisioning calls provision_tenant(tenant_id_str) for each candidate (services.py:154). But provision_tenant short-circuits for any status not in PENDING/PROVISIONING (services.py:212-220): it logs provision_skipped_unexpected_status and returns without doing work. The repair loop then re-reads the tenant (services.py:155-163); still ACTIVE with empty container fields, ready is False, so it is counted as incomplete and increments summary['failed']. An ACTIVE tenant with empty container metadata is therefore structurally un-repairable by this sweep and reports failed on every run, with no path that re-provisions it.

The intent-vs-behavior mismatch is real and internal to this code: the author explicitly placed ACTIVE in scope (a healthy ACTIVE tenant is never swept, since it has both container fields), signaling the sweep is meant to repair corrupted-ACTIVE rows, yet provision_tenant refuses to act on them. Not a misread, not guarded upstream, not handled elsewhere.

Reachability of the ACTIVE+empty state is low, which is why low severity is correct. Normal provisioning sets container_id/fqdn and status=ACTIVE in one atomic save (services.py:355-371), so it can't arise there; deprovision clears the fields but sets DELETED not ACTIVE (services.py:746-748). The one path that flips to ACTIVE without re-verifying container fields is reactivation: handle_checkout_completed flips SUSPENDED->ACTIVE on the assumption a hibernated container exists (billing/services.py:333-337). A suspended tenant normally retains its container fields, so reaching ACTIVE+empty requires prior corruption — but that is exactly the corruption class the repair sweep names itself to fix. No regression (this is original design), so it is a partial/incomplete-fix gap: the tool advertises (via ACTIVE-inclusion) repair of stale-ACTIVE tenants but cannot deliver it, leaving such a tenant's assistant unreachable until an operator manually intervenes.
suggested_fix: Make the repair path able to act on the ACTIVE-but-incomplete case. Two reasonable options: (1) In provision_tenant, broaden the status guard so that an ACTIVE tenant missing container_id/container_fqdn is treated as re-provisionable (e.g. allow ACTIVE when container fields are empty, resetting status to PROVISIONING before the create flow). (2) Alternatively, in repair_stale_tenant_provisioning, demote a matched ACTIVE+empty tenant to PROVISIONING (or PENDING) before calling provision_tenant, so the existing guard accepts it. Option 2 is the smaller, more contained change and keeps provision_tenant's invariant intact. Either way, add a test covering an ACTIVE tenant with empty container fields running through the sweep and ending repaired.

detail: Confirmed real, but cosmetic and edge-case-bounded. When the container tool-call hook reports progress without a client_msg_id, ChatProgressEventView (chat_views.py:608-609) narrates the tenant's newest PENDING AppChatMessage via order_by('-created_at'). The comment at chat_views.py:603-607 justifies this by claiming "Per-tenant turns are serialized (one container)" so the newest PENDING IS the in-flight one. That justification is wrong about scope: the pending queue serializes per (tenant, channel, channel_user_id), and for iOS channel_user_id = thread.id (chat_views.py:234). The per-key single-turn lease (pending_queue.py:481-489) and the FIFO claim (pending_queue.py:491-506: order_by('created_at'), head=rows[0]) are BOTH per-thread, not per-tenant. Decisively, pending_queue.py:1384 states "thread:<id> so each ChatThread is its own OpenClaw session", so two different threads route to two different sessions and can genuinely be in-flight concurrently in the same container (they do NOT trigger the "live session already handling a turn" rejection, which is per-session). Siri-escalated turns use _get_or_create_main_thread (siri_views.py:217), so they share the main-thread key and coalesce safely; the real exposure is cross-thread. Concrete failure: thread A's turn is the older PENDING row and is actually in flight; the user sends on thread B, creating a newer PENDING row; the container's client_msg_id-less progress event lands on thread B. A user polling thread A sees a dead spinner while thread B shows premature 'thinking/searching your journal', and the iOS Live Activity can map to the wrong activity. No data corruption: phase/phase_detail is cosmetic, gets overwritten when thread B's turn actually starts, and self-corrects once the tenant drains to one PENDING turn. Reachability is narrow (single human user must have 2+ threads PENDING at once AND the runtime must emit a client_msg_id-less progress event), which is why severity is low, but the defect is genuine and the in-code comment actively asserts the inverse of the FIFO drain it relies on.
suggested_fix: Make the no-client_msg_id fallback pick the actually-in-flight turn instead of the newest. The in-flight turn corresponds to the OLDEST PENDING AppChatMessage whose thread currently holds a live PendingMessage in-flight lease. Simplest correct change: order by created_at ascending (oldest first) to match the FIFO drain, OR (better) join through PendingMessage: select the PENDING AppChatMessage(s) for thread ids that have a PendingMessage with delivery_in_flight_until > now, and narrate the oldest of those. Update the comment at chat_views.py:603-607 to state serialization is per-thread/session (not per-tenant) and that the in-flight turn is the oldest leased one. Update the test test_updates_latest_pending_when_no_client_msg_id (test_siri_views.py:309-328) accordingly. Cleanest long-term fix is to have the runtime hook carry the thread/session id so narration targets the correct thread directly.

detail:
The pattern is real and accurately described. EntityRegistryItemView.patch (apps/tenants/views.py:1090-1145) and .delete (1158-1172) read tenant.pii_entity_map, mutate the dict in Python, and write the ENTIRE field back via Tenant.objects.filter(pk=...).update(pii_entity_map=entity_map) with no select_for_update, no transaction.atomic, and no JSONB-merge expression. The inbound redactor does the symmetric thing: apps/pii/redactor.py:292 reads existing_map = tenant.pii_entity_map (a snapshot from tenant-load time, before the slow NER step), then at lines 412-414 — only when it mints NEW entities — writes merged = {**existing_map, **new_map_entries} over the whole field. I verified there are no locks/atomic/F() expressions in either path. So the race is genuine: if a message is being redacted (its existing_map snapshot still contains placeholder X) and that message mints a new entity, the write-back resurrects X, silently undoing a concurrent user DELETE of X or reverting a concurrent PATCH that corrected X's name. The PIIDenylist add/remove/bulk views (1244-1259, 1278-1287, 1329-1353) have the identical unguarded full-dict overwrite of pii_denylist, so an 'ignore this person' action can be lost the same way. When it fires, the symptom is exactly the privacy failure the People page exists to fix (a corrected/deleted real name reappears and keeps leaking into replies). I downgraded severity from medium to low because the trigger is narrow: the user must be editing the privacy page within the read-modify-write window of a single inbound message AND that message must mint at least one new entity (the redactor does NOT write back when none are found); it is single-tenant, non-corrupting across rows, and user-recoverable (re-delete). The wide NER latency between read and write does widen the window somewhat, so it is not purely theoretical — just low-frequency.</final_detail>
<suggested_fix>Make each writer a row-locked or atomic read-modify-write so the curate-vs-redact writers compose instead of clobbering. Two practical options: (1) wrap each mutation in transaction.atomic() with Tenant.objects.select_for_update().get(pk=...) to re-read the latest map under a row lock, apply the delta, then save only the field — applied to both the views and the redactor write at redactor.py:412-414; or (2) move the merge into the database with a JSONB expression so Postgres does the merge server-side (e.g. for adds, pii_entity_map = pii_entity_map || new_entries via a Func/RawSQL, and for the view DELETE, pii_entity_map - placeholder). Option 2 also fixes the redactor cheaply since it is purely additive. The views' DELETE/PATCH still need the lock-and-re-read (or the '-' / jsonb_set operators) because they remove/replace keys. The denylist views need the same treatment.</suggested_fix>
<fix_complexity>small</fix_complexity>
<evidence">apps/tenants/views.py:1145 — PATCH writes whole map: Tenant.objects.filter(pk=tenant.pk).update(pii_entity_map=entity_map), no lock/atomic</evidence>
</invoke>
